When active, it obscures the review header, body, sidebar, and comments, but respects the same left and right gutters as the rest of the layout, not covering the full browser edge-to-edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned absolutely or fixed within the central content region, expanding to fill its width and height and sitting above all other components with a high z-index while loading; removed entirely from the flow once content is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review header sits at the top of the left (main) column, directly beneath the breadcrumb-and-back-link bar and aligned flush with the main column’s left edge. It spans the full width of the main column (about two-thirds of the central container) and contains the review title and any key summary info. There is a comfortable vertical gap (around 16–24px) above it from the breadcrumb bar, and a similar gap below before the metadata panel. The title is visually dominant, occupying one to three lines, with generous whitespace above and below, echoing the editorial style of a blog post headline. If a small thumbnail or cover image is part of the header, it appears below the title, left-aligned, and constrained to the column width, with 16px padding or margin separating it from the text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow, anchoring the top of the main column content; it scrolls out of view with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review-meta-panel is positioned immediately below the review header in the main column, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 8–16px. It spans the full width of the main column and appears as a low-height horizontal band. On the left side, it displays metadata such as the review date, category, rating, and possibly a compact breadcrumb trail; on the right side of the same row there may be secondary information such as comment count or reading time, aligned to the right edge of the column. Text is smaller and lighter than the title, reinforcing its secondary status. Overall height is determined by one or two lines of text, with about 8–12px padding above and below, keeping the band visually subtle and unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline in the vertical flow of the main column, with no sticky or floating behavior; it compresses naturally on smaller screens, stacking metadata items vertically if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review body occupies the central, most spacious portion of the left column, starting roughly 24–32px below the interaction controls and extending downward as a continuous block of text and inline media. It is full-width within the main column, with generous internal spacing between paragraphs (around 12–16px) and any subheadings (24px above, 8–12px below). Images embedded in the body, such as a book cover or movie still, are centered or left-aligned within the column and do not spill into the sidebar. Line length is kept comfortably short by the main column width, mirroring a traditional editorial article. There is a clear vertical separation (around 32px) between the end of the body and the start of the comments section to indicate a shift from editorial content to interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and block-level, flowing naturally with the page scroll and defining the dominant vertical rhythm of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The author-link component sits at the top of the right sidebar, aligned horizontally with approximately the midpoint between the review header and the meta panel in the main column, so that both columns feel visually aligned near the top. It occupies the full width of the sidebar (about one-third of the central container). The component is presented as a simple panel or text block with an 'Author' or 'About the reviewer' heading at the top, followed by a brief descriptive line and a prominent inline link or button leading to the author’s page. The panel has internal padding around 16–24px on all sides and is separated from the main content by a 24px horizontal gutter. Below it, a vertical gap of about 24px is left for potential future sidebar sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar column; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review-interaction-bar is placed directly below the review-meta-panel in the main column, with a vertical gap of about 16px. It spans the full width of the main column but is visually light, with a height defined by a single row of icons and/or text buttons (e.g., Like, Add to List). Elements are aligned left-to-right, starting from the left edge, with 16px horizontal spacing between controls. The right end of the bar is left mostly empty to maintain a clean, minimalist look. A faint divider line above and/or below the bar can be used to subtly separate it from the header and body without creating a heavy card. A small 16px gap separates it from the reading-list-selector placed immediately beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the main column content; it remains in place within the scroll and does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-selector sits immediately below the review-interaction-bar in the main column, separated by about 8–16px of vertical space. It shares the same column width and aligns flush with the left and right edges of the main content. The control may appear as a dropdown, pill selector, or small panel listing existing reading lists; regardless of format, it should be relatively compact (one to three lines tall), with 12–16px of internal padding to keep it readable. This placement visually ties membership management to the interaction bar above while still staying above the main body text. There is a slightly larger gap (around 24px) below this component before the review body begins, reinforcing the transition from controls to reading content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the vertical flow in the main column; it compresses down the column on smaller screens, stacking any internal controls vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comments-section occupies a full-width block at the bottom of the main column, beginning roughly 32px below the end of the review body. It is visually grouped as a distinct section with a 'Comments' heading at the top, aligned left and spanning the width of the main column. Inside this section, the comment-list appears first, followed by the comments-pagination and then the comment-form stacked vertically. The section maintains 16–24px internal padding on all sides, echoing the spacing used in other major content blocks. The overall width matches the main column, and the right sidebar does not intrude into this area; the comments stretch vertically downward as needed, keeping the same vertical alignment with the sidebar’s right edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned after the review body in the document flow; it scrolls normally and does not pin to any specific viewport position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment-list is the first subsection within the comments-section, located directly below the 'Comments' heading with around 16px of vertical spacing. It fills the width of the main column inside the comments-section padding. Individual comments are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between them, each comment typically spanning the full width of the available space. Comment metadata (author, date) is placed at the top of each comment block, followed by the comment text. The overall list is allowed to grow vertically, and a slightly larger gap (16–24px) is left at the bottom of the last comment before the pagination controls begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the comments-section, expanding vertically as needed; it has no independent scrolling or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comments-pagination control sits directly below the comment-list within the comments-section, separated by a vertical gap of about 16px. It spans the width of the main column but is visually compact, occupying a single row. Pagination links or buttons are horizontally centered or left-aligned depending on the site’s convention, with 8–12px of horizontal spacing between page numbers or navigation arrows. There is another modest gap (16–24px) between the pagination and the comment-form that follows, visually indicating the end of the current comment batch before the form to add a new one.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow of the comments-section; it scrolls with the rest of the page and does not float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment-form occupies the bottom portion of the comments-section, starting about 16–24px below the comments-pagination. It spans the full width of the main column within the section’s padding. Fields (name, email, textarea, submit button) are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing between them, maintaining the page’s overall spacious but functional density. The textarea is the largest field, with enough height for comfortable typing, and the submit button is left-aligned under it. A slightly larger bottom padding (around 24px) provides a comfortable visual endpoint to the entire comments area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and block-level, concluding the comments-section; it scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-and-back-link component is the first element inside the central content container, positioned directly below the global site header and above the two-column layout. It spans the full width of the central container (70–75% of the viewport), aligning with both the main column and sidebar below. On the left, a small back arrow/link (e.g., '← Back to Reviews') is placed, with a subtle breadcrumb trail possibly extending to the right of it. The text is small and understated, with 8–12px padding above and below, forming a slim band that gently anchors the user without competing with the review title. A 16–24px vertical gap separates this bar from the review-header below, visually decoupling navigation from content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sitting at the top of the main content flow; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page, with no sticky behavior.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays review search results for a query with filters and sorting, and preserves state when navigating to and from review detail pages.

core_features: Execute review searches using the query and optional filters via the searchReviews interface, and render paginated results.
Load available filter options (genres, tags, rating ranges) for the current search context via the getReviewFilterOptions interface.
Allow users to filter search results by content type, genre, tags, and rating using controls that update URL parameters and re-run searchReviews.
Allow users to change the sort order of search results using a sort control that updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-runs searchReviews.
Provide pagination controls that update the page (and optionally pageSize) URL parameters and re-run searchReviews.
Provide clickable result titles that navigate to the review detail page while preserving a returnUrl parameter that restores the current search state when the user goes back.
Show the active query and applied filters in a summary area, allowing quick removal of individual filters by updating URL parameters and re-running searchReviews.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Run a new search from any page
1. User enters a search term into the global search form located in the site header.
2. User submits the form; browser navigates to search.html with the query URL parameter set and other search-related parameters either omitted or preserved according to the form behavior.
3. On page load, Search Results reads the query parameter (and any other present parameters such as content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating, sortBy, page, pageSize).
4. The page calls getReviewFilterOptions with a context value representing the search page and a contentType derived from the content_type URL parameter (or a neutral value when no content_type filter is applied).
5. The page calls searchReviews with the query and a filters object constructed from the URL parameters, plus sortBy, page, and pageSize.
6. When searchReviews resolves, the results list, total count, pagination, and applied filter summary are rendered.
7. User reviews the results and, if desired, uses filters, sorting, or pagination to refine or browse further.

Workflow: Refine search using filters
1. User is on search.html with results already displayed for a given query.
2. User interacts with the filter sidebar or filter controls (e.g., selects a content type option, chooses a genre, selects one or more tags, or adjusts the rating range).
3. The filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters (content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating) and resets page to the first page, while preserving the current query, sortBy, and pageSize parameters.
4. The browser either updates the URL and re-renders the page or the page logic detects the URL change; searchReviews is called again with the updated filters, and getReviewFilterOptions may be re-called if the content_type filter changed.
5. The results list, total count, pagination, and applied filter summary update to reflect the new filters.
6. User may remove filters by interacting with applied filter chips or clearing controls, which removes or alters the corresponding URL parameters and triggers the same refresh behavior.

Workflow: Change sort order of results
1. User is viewing search results and wants to change how they are ordered.
2. User interacts with the sort control (e.g., selects a different sort option).
3. The sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and resets page to the first page while preserving query, filters, and pageSize.
4. The page calls searchReviews with the new sortBy value and existing filters.
5. Results are re-rendered in the new order, with the sort control reflecting the current sortBy parameter.

Workflow: Navigate between pages of results
1. User is viewing search results with pagination controls visible.
2. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., a specific page number or next/previous control).
3. The pagination component updates the page URL parameter to the selected page number while preserving query, filters, sortBy, and pageSize.
4. The page calls searchReviews with the new page value.
5. Results list re-renders to show the correct subset of items for the requested page, and pagination controls update their active state.

Workflow: Open a review and return to the same search state
1. User is on search.html, has a specific combination of query, filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize set via URL parameters.
2. User clicks the title of a specific search result.
3. The result link navigates to review_detail.html with parameters: id set to the reviewId from the search result, from set to the literal value 'search', and returnUrl set to the fully constructed Search Results URL including all current parameters.
4. On the review detail page, when the user activates the 'Back to results' action, review_detail.html navigates to the URL contained in returnUrl.
5. When search.html loads from this returnUrl, it parses the URL parameters, calls searchReviews (and getReviewFilterOptions as needed), and restores the exact search state (query, filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize) as when the user left.

Workflow: Adjust page size (if supported by the UI)
1. User is on search.html and wants to see more or fewer results per page.
2. User interacts with a page-size control.
3. The control updates the pageSize URL parameter and resets page to the first page while preserving query, filters, and sortBy.
4. The page calls searchReviews with the new pageSize and updated page.
5. The results list and pagination controls re-render to reflect the new number of results per page.
interactions: URL-driven page load: When search.html is loaded, client logic reads query, content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating, sortBy, page, and pageSize from the URL. If query is present, the page calls searchReviews with these values. The presence or absence of filters and sortBy directly determines the filters and sorting passed to searchReviews.

Filter change in sidebar: When the user selects or changes a content type filter, the filter component updates the content_type URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and optionally re-requests filter options via getReviewFilterOptions if content_type affects available options. Then the page calls searchReviews with the updated content_type filter.

Genre filter interaction: Selecting a genre sets or updates the genre URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers a new call to searchReviews. Deselecting or clearing the genre removes the genre parameter and triggers another call to searchReviews.

Tag filter interaction: Selecting or deselecting tags updates the tag_ids URL parameter as a comma-separated list of tag IDs. This update resets page to the first page and triggers a new searchReviews call. Applied tag filters also appear as removable chips that, when clicked, remove the corresponding tag ID from tag_ids and re-run searchReviews.

Rating filter interaction: Setting or adjusting rating filters updates the min_rating and/or max_rating URL parameters based on the user’s selection (for example, selecting a rating bucket or adjusting a range control). The change resets page to the first page and re-runs searchReviews with the updated rating filters.

Sort control interaction: Changing the sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers a new searchReviews call. The sort control reflects the current sortBy value from the URL and remains in sync with URL changes.

Pagination interaction: Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter (and may keep pageSize unchanged or update it if the control supports it). After the URL change, searchReviews is called with the new page, and results are updated accordingly.

Applied filter chip removal: Clicking an applied filter chip (e.g., for content type, genre, tag, or rating) updates the URL by removing or altering the relevant parameter (content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating) and resets page to the first page. searchReviews is then re-called to display results without the removed filter.

Result item navigation: Clicking a result title constructs a URL to review_detail.html that includes id, from, and returnUrl parameters, derived from the clicked result data and the current search.html URL. Navigation is entirely via URL parameters without additional API calls.

Empty state interaction: If searchReviews returns no items for the current combination of query and filters, the page displays an empty state. Interactions in this state (such as clearing filters or modifying the query via the header search form) update URL parameters and trigger searchReviews again.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is entirely determined by URL parameters and the responses from getReviewFilterOptions and searchReviews. The key URL parameters are: query, content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating, sortBy, page, and pageSize.

Initialization:
- On every load of search.html, the page reads all defined URL parameters.
- If query is not present or empty, the page may choose not to call searchReviews until the user submits a search; otherwise, it constructs a filters object from content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, and max_rating and calls searchReviews immediately.
- For getReviewFilterOptions, the page uses a context string representing the search results use case and a contentType argument derived from the content_type URL parameter (or a neutral value when content_type is not set) to retrieve filter metadata.

Mapping URL parameters to searchReviews:
- query → searchReviews.query.
- content_type → searchReviews.filters.contentType (if the parameter is present).
- genre → searchReviews.filters.genre (if present).
- tag_ids → split by comma into an array and assigned to searchReviews.filters.tagIds (if present and non-empty).
- min_rating → searchReviews.filters.minRating (if present).
- max_rating → searchReviews.filters.maxRating (if present).
- sortBy → searchReviews.sortBy. If sortBy is omitted, searchReviews relies on its default sorting behavior.
- page → searchReviews.page. If page is omitted, the page logic uses a default page index (for example, the first page) when calling searchReviews.
- pageSize → searchReviews.pageSize. If pageSize is omitted, a default page size is used when calling searchReviews.

URL updates and navigation:
- Every user interaction that changes filters, sorting, or pagination updates the URL parameters accordingly, either via navigation or history API usage, ensuring that the URL always reflects the current state.
- When any of content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating, sortBy, or pageSize change based on user input, page is reset to the first page before re-running searchReviews, and the page parameter is updated in the URL.
- Removing a filter removes its corresponding parameter from the URL entirely rather than setting a fixed placeholder value, so that the absence of the parameter clearly indicates that the filter is not applied.

Back-navigation preservation:
- When building links to review_detail.html, the page constructs returnUrl by taking the current search.html URL (including all relevant parameters) as-is, ensuring that every aspect of the search state (query, content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating, sortBy, page, and pageSize if present) is preserved.
- Returning from review_detail.html by navigating to returnUrl reinitializes search.html with exactly the same URL parameters, which causes searchReviews (and getReviewFilterOptions, if needed) to run with the same inputs and reconstruct the previous state without relying on any client-side storage.

Deep-linking:
- Users or external systems can link directly to a specific search state by constructing search.html URLs that include any subset of the defined parameters. When such a URL is opened, the page reads the parameters and calls searchReviews in the same way as for a state reached through in-page interactions, resulting in the same search state being reproduced purely from the URL.

components.functionality: Displays the current search query and total result count, and may show a brief summary of applied filters. It binds to the query URL parameter and the totalCount returned by searchReviews, and updates whenever the URL parameters or searchReviews response change.

Loads available filter options using getReviewFilterOptions and renders controls for content type, genre, tags, and rating. When the user changes any filter, it updates the corresponding URL parameters (content_type, genre, tag_ids, min_rating, max_rating), resets page to the first page, and causes searchReviews to be called with updated filters.

Shows the currently applied filters (content type, genre, selected tags, rating range) as removable chips or labels based on the current URL parameters and the metadata from getReviewFilterOptions. Removing a chip updates the corresponding URL parameter (or removes it), resets page to the first page, and triggers a new call to searchReviews.

Provides a control to select the sort order for search results. The selected option is synchronized with the sortBy URL parameter. When the user changes the sort option, the component updates sortBy in the URL, resets page to the first page, and triggers a new call to searchReviews with the chosen sortBy value.

Renders a list of search results returned from searchReviews.items, including title, snippet, content type, genre, rating, publication date, thumbnail, author, and tags as provided. Each item’s title links to review_detail.html with id, from, and returnUrl parameters constructed from the result data and current URL state. If no items are returned, it displays an empty-state message and suggests adjusting the query or filters.

Displays pagination controls (e.g., page numbers and navigation links) based on totalCount, page, and pageSize from searchReviews. When a user selects a different page, it updates the page URL parameter while preserving query, filters, sortBy, and pageSize, then triggers a new searchReviews call.

Optionally allows the user to select how many results to display per page. The selected size is synchronized with the pageSize URL parameter. When the user changes the page size, the component updates pageSize in the URL, resets page to the first page, and triggers searchReviews with the new pageSize.

Coordinates reading URL parameters, calling getReviewFilterOptions and searchReviews, and updating child components. On initialization and whenever URL parameters change, it constructs the correct filters and options, invokes getReviewFilterOptions with the appropriate context and contentType, invokes searchReviews with query, filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize, and propagates the results and filter options to other components.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width central content area that likely maps to a 12-column grid with an approximate 8/4 split between main content and sidebar. The content column appears centered with equal light-grey gutters on each side.

Within each column, content flows in a simple top-to-bottom stack. In the main column, the order is: search query summary, applied filters bar, a compact control row combining page size and sort selector, the vertical list of search results, and finally pagination. In the sidebar, the search filters panel sits near the top and fills downward as needed.

Within each column, content flows in a simple top-to-bottom stack. In the main column, the order is: search query summary, applied filters bar, a compact control row combining page size and sort selector, the vertical list of search results, and finally pagination. In the sidebar, the search filters panel sits near the top and fills downward as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content band occupies roughly 70–75% of the viewport width and is centered, leaving light grey gutters of equal width on the far left and right. Inside this band, the main search column uses about two-thirds of the width (approximately 8 columns in a 12-column mental grid) and the filter sidebar uses about one-third (approximately 4 columns). Vertical spacing follows the mockup’s rhythm: 24–32px (3–4 base units) between major blocks like the query summary, filters bar, controls, results list, and pagination; 16px (2 units) between smaller elements. Internal padding within panels (especially the filters sidebar) is 16–24px on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is low-to-medium, favoring comfortable reading. The main column keeps a relatively narrow text measure suitable for body copy, and results are separated with 16–24px vertical spacing so each result feels distinct but still part of a continuous list. The filter sidebar is slightly denser, with filter groups stacked vertically but given at least 8–12px between controls and 16px above headings to avoid visual clutter. Overall whitespace around each component is generous and consistent with the blog-like editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a familiar F-pattern. After scanning the site header and navigation, attention drops to the top-left of the content band where the search query summary anchors the page. From there, the eye moves downward through the applied filters bar and the compact row of search controls, then into the vertical flow of results. The right-hand filter sidebar is aligned with the top of the main content, acting as a peripheral but accessible column that users glance to when they need to refine results, then return to the left column to continue scanning. Links and interactive states use the single accent color to create subtle visual anchors along this flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width site header and navigation, a centered content strip occupies roughly three-quarters of the page, framed by light grey gutters on either side. This strip is split into a classic two-column layout: a wider left column that feels like the main article space, and a narrower right column echoing a traditional blog sidebar. At the top of the left column, a text-centric summary line states the search query and result count, establishing context. Just beneath it, a slim applied-filters bar spans the width of the main column, showing active filters as understated chips. A compact control row follows, with page-size options aligned to the left and sort controls aligned to the right, forming a thin horizontal band framing the top of the results. Below this, the search results list cascades vertically down the page like a sequence of short review excerpts, each separated by comfortable white space. At the bottom of the main column, pagination controls sit with breathing room above them, marking the end of the current result set. To the right, the filter sidebar begins at roughly the same vertical starting line as the query summary, with clean headings and neatly spaced filter groups that echo the original ‘Author’ and ‘Archives’ blocks from the mockup. The overall effect is a calm, editorial layout where the main result list clearly dominates, and filters and controls are present but visually quiet, supporting an easy top-to-bottom reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search query summary sits at the very top of the main (left) column, directly beneath the site’s global header and navigation, and aligned flush with the left edge of the main content column. It spans the full width of this main column, leaving equal side gutters only at the outer edges of the central content band. There is a generous top margin of about 32–48px from the bottom of the global header to this summary, reinforcing its role as the first element of the search results context. The text is left-aligned, occupying a single line or two at most, with comfortable line spacing. Beneath the summary, there is approximately 16–24px of vertical space before the applied filters bar begins, maintaining the mockup’s spacious editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the normal document flow within the main column; scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search filters panel occupies the right-hand sidebar, mirroring the position of the ‘Author’ and ‘Archives’ sections in the original mockup. Its top edge is vertically aligned with the first line of the search query summary in the main column, preserving a clean horizontal start line across both columns. The panel consumes roughly one-third of the central content width, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides to create a card-like, but not heavily boxed, feeling. Inside, filter groups are stacked vertically: each group begins with a small heading aligned left, followed by related controls (checkboxes, dropdowns, or links) with 8–12px vertical spacing between items and about 16px above each new heading. The panel extends downward only as far as its content requires, leaving open white space beneath it if the results list is taller. A consistent right gutter separates it from the browser edge, matching the left gutter on the opposite side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar content within the central column layout; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied filters bar is placed directly below the search query summary within the main column, maintaining a 16–24px vertical gap between the two. It spans the full width of the main column, forming a low-profile horizontal strip that visually reads as a subtle band of filter chips or labels. The content is left-aligned, with applied filters arranged in a row that wraps to multiple lines if necessary, maintaining at least 8px of horizontal and vertical spacing between chips. The bar has slightly more top and bottom padding (about 8–12px) than a simple text line, making it distinct but still light. There is another 16–24px gap below this bar before the control row (page size and sort) begins, so the layout does not feel compressed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component within the main column’s flow; expands vertically if many filters are applied, pushing the controls and results downward accordingly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search sort control sits in the main column just above the results list, sharing a horizontal control row with the page size selector. This row appears below the applied filters bar, separated by roughly 16–24px. Within this row, the sort control is aligned to the right edge of the main column, with its label and dropdown or segmented control arranged horizontally. The control’s vertical height is modest, matching the overall compact feel of the mockup’s meta information (like date and comments). There is 8–12px of vertical padding above and below this row to visually separate it from both the filters bar above and the first result below. The sort control does not dominate visually; it appears as a small, right-anchored control at the top of the results stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main column; stays at the top of the visible results area and scrolls away with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search results list occupies the bulk of the main (left) column and is the tallest element on the page. It begins immediately beneath the control row (page size on the left, sort on the right), with about 16–24px of vertical separation. The list runs vertically down the column, respecting the fixed-width text measure of the editorial layout. Each result appears as a distinct block: title at the top, followed by brief meta information (such as review type, date, rating) and a short excerpt. These elements within each result are left-aligned, with 4–8px spacing between lines and 16–24px between separate result items. No heavy borders are necessary; separation is achieved primarily through vertical whitespace and consistent typography. The results list stops above the pagination controls, leaving about 24–32px of space, which makes the pagination feel like a clear endpoint for the current set.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically based on the number of results per page; scrolls as the main reading area, maintaining the dominant vertical flow of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned at the bottom of the main column, directly below the last search result, separated by approximately 24–32px of whitespace. They are horizontally centered within the main column, creating a subtle visual anchor that signals the end of the current results batch. The controls themselves are compact (page numbers, previous/next arrows), with 8–12px spacing between buttons or links and minimal but consistent padding inside each clickable area. This cluster forms a low-profile band, visually lighter than a full-width bar, allowing it to be noticeable without competing with result titles. There is a modest amount of space (24–32px) below the pagination before the end of the content area, keeping the bottom of the page from feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the main column; it appears after the last result and moves up or down depending on the current page’s result count.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page size control shares the same horizontal row as the sort control, positioned just above the search results list and beneath the applied filters bar. Within this row, it is aligned to the left edge of the main column, directly mirroring the sort control on the right. The label and selector (e.g., a dropdown or segmented buttons for 10/20/50 items) are laid out in a left-to-right sequence with small horizontal spacing (8–12px). The control’s height matches the sort control so that the row reads as a single, cohesive band of controls. This band does not span the full height of a typical button bar; instead it sits lightly between the filters bar and the results, maintaining 8–12px of vertical padding above and below for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element embedded in the main column’s control row; adjusting the page size changes the vertical length of the results list below but does not change its own position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search state manager is a non-visual, underlying controller that has no explicit rendered footprint in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind or around the central content area, managing the synchronization of search query, filters, sort order, page size, and pagination state. It ensures that when a user navigates to a review detail page and returns, the previously chosen filters and scroll position in the results are preserved. There is no visible panel, no dedicated spacing, and no impact on the page’s visual composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical/controller layer; not part of the spatial layout. It operates invisibly and does not occupy or reserve any physical space on the page.

name: Reading Lists Overview
title: Reading Lists
description: Overview page for viewing, creating, renaming, and deleting reading lists, and navigating to individual list detail pages.

core_features: Load and display all reading lists for the current user using getUserReadingLists, including any built-in and custom lists.
Provide a form to create new custom reading lists using createReadingList, with name and optional description.
Allow navigation to a specific list’s detail page (list_detail.html) when a list row is clicked, passing the listId as a URL parameter.
Allow renaming and updating the description of existing custom reading lists using updateReadingList.
Allow deleting custom reading lists (but not default lists) using deleteReadingList, with confirmation.
Provide feedback for loading, validation errors, and API errors when list operations are performed.
user_workflows:
View all reading lists:
1. User navigates to reading_lists.html via header navigation or direct URL.
2. On page load, the page calls getUserReadingLists with no parameters.
3. While waiting, the page shows a loading state.
4. On success, the page displays all lists returned (using fields such as name, listType, description, isDefault, itemsCount).
5. If getUserReadingLists returns an error, the page shows an error message and a retry action that calls getUserReadingLists again.

Open a specific list:
1. User locates a list in the overview list (from getUserReadingLists).
2. User clicks the list row or a dedicated "Open" interaction on that row.
3. The page navigates the browser to list_detail.html with a query parameter listId set to the clicked list’s id (e.g., list_detail.html?listId=<id_from_API>).

Create a new custom list:
1. User locates the "Create New List" form on reading_lists.html.
2. User enters a list name and, optionally, a description.
3. User submits the form.
4. Client validates that the name is non-empty before calling the API.
5. If valid, the page calls createReadingList with the name and description values.
6. While the request is in progress, the form shows a submitting state and prevents duplicate submissions.
7. On success (success = true), the new list from the response is appended to the in-memory list collection and rendered in the overview without reloading the page.
8. Optionally, the URL can be updated to include createdListId=<new_list_id> so the new list can be highlighted.
9. On failure (success = false or network error), the form displays the returned message or a generic error and allows retry.

Rename or update description of a custom list:
1. User identifies a list to rename in the overview.
2. User clicks a "Rename" or "Edit" action for that list.
3. The page opens an inline edit state or a dedicated edit form populated with the current name and description from the list data.
4. User modifies the name and/or description and submits.
5. The page calls updateReadingList with listId set to the list’s id, and name/description set to the new values.
6. While the request is in progress, the edit UI shows a saving state and disables repeated submission.
7. On success (success = true), the list’s displayed name/description are updated using the list object returned by updateReadingList, and the edit UI closes.
8. On failure, the error message is shown, and the user can retry or cancel.

Delete a custom list:
1. User identifies a list that can be deleted (listType and/or isDefault indicate whether deletion is allowed; delete UI is only shown for lists that are not default).
2. User clicks the "Delete" action for that list.
3. A confirmation dialog or confirmation step appears, summarizing which list will be deleted.
4. If the user confirms, the page calls deleteReadingList with listId set to the list’s id.
5. While the request is in progress, the confirmation UI shows a deleting state.
6. On success (success = true), the list is removed from the in-memory collection and no longer shown in the overview.
7. On failure, the page shows the returned message or a generic error and keeps the list visible.

Refresh lists after list operations:
1. After a create, update, or delete operation, the page either updates the local list state from the API response or, optionally, re-fetches all lists via getUserReadingLists to ensure consistency.
2. If a full refresh is used, the page calls getUserReadingLists again and replaces the currently displayed lists with the new result.
interactions:
Page load:
- Event: Browser loads reading_lists.html.
- Action: Trigger initial data fetch via getUserReadingLists.
- State: Show loading indicator until response is received; then render lists or show error.

Click list row to open detail:
- Event: User clicks on a list row or its "Open" control.
- Action: Navigate to list_detail.html?listId=<id> using the id from the clicked list.
- Result: User is taken to the list detail page to view and manage items in that list.

Create New List form interactions:
- onChange: Update local form state for name and description.
- onSubmit: Validate required fields, then call createReadingList(name, description).
- onSuccess: Add returned list to local list collection, optionally update URL with createdListId, clear form fields, and show success feedback.
- onError: Display error message; keep user input intact for correction.

Rename/Edit list interactions:
- onClick (Edit/Rename): Open edit UI for the selected list, using its current name and description.
- onChange: Track changes in name/description fields in local state.
- onSubmit: Call updateReadingList(listId, name, description).
- onSuccess: Replace list properties in the local collection with values from response.list and close edit UI.
- onError: Show error, keep edit UI open for retry or cancel.

Delete list interactions:
- onClick (Delete): Open a confirmation UI for the selected list; this is only available for lists where deletion is allowed (e.g., isDefault is false).
- onConfirm: Call deleteReadingList(listId).
- onSuccess: Remove the list from local collection and close confirmation dialog.
- onError: Show error message; keep list visible and allow user to retry later.
- onCancel: Close confirmation UI without calling the API.

Retry loading lists:
- Event: User clicks a "Retry" control after an error loading lists.
- Action: Call getUserReadingLists again and update the list view.

Client-side validation for list creation and renaming:
- Event: User attempts to submit with invalid input (e.g., empty name).
- Action: Prevent API call, display inline validation messages, and focus the invalid field.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page has no required incoming URL parameters but can use optional query parameters to control or reflect UI state. All persistent state is kept either in memory during the session or encoded in the URL; localStorage is not used.

URL parameters:
- createdListId (optional): If present (e.g., reading_lists.html?createdListId=<id>), the page will, after loading lists via getUserReadingLists, attempt to locate the list whose id matches createdListId. If found, that list can be programmatically highlighted or scrolled into view to indicate a recently created list. This parameter is not required and is ignored if no matching list is found.
- editListId (optional): If present (e.g., reading_lists.html?editListId=<id>), after getUserReadingLists completes, the page enters edit mode for the matching list (if any), opening the rename/edit UI automatically. If no matching list exists, the parameter is ignored.
- status (optional): Can be used to show transient feedback after redirects from other pages (e.g., reading_lists.html?status=deleted or status=error). The page reads this parameter on load and displays an appropriate status banner; after rendering the banner, client code may remove or ignore the parameter to avoid repeated messages on manual reload.

State transitions:
- loadingState: Initially set to 'loading' on page load; set to 'loaded' on successful getUserReadingLists, or 'error' on failure.
- listsState: In-memory collection representing the array returned by getUserReadingLists, updated after createReadingList, updateReadingList, and deleteReadingList calls. This state is the single source of truth for rendered lists.
- formState (create/edit): Separate in-memory objects hold form field values, validation errors, and submission state (idle, submitting, success, error). These states never persist via localStorage; if needed across reloads, they are re-derived from URL parameters.
- selectionState: editListId and createdListId from the URL are read on load and mapped to the corresponding lists, controlling which list is in edit mode and which (if any) is highlighted.

components.functionality: Initializes the Reading Lists page by fetching all reading lists for the current user and coordinating loading/error state and URL-driven behavior (createdListId, editListId).

Displays the collection of reading lists returned by getUserReadingLists, including fields such as name, listType, description, isDefault, itemsCount, and provides per-list actions (open, edit, delete) depending on list properties.

Provides inputs for creating a new custom reading list (name required, description optional), validates user input, and submits the data via createReadingList. On success, integrates the new list into the existing overview without a full page reload.

Allows renaming an existing list and updating its description. It is initialized with values from the selected ReadingList item and sends updates via updateReadingList. It can be activated either by a user click or automatically when editListId is present in the URL.

Confirms whether a user wants to delete a selected custom list. It calls deleteReadingList when the user confirms and removes the list from the overview on success. It is only invoked for lists where deletion is allowed, based on their properties (for example, isDefault being false).

Displays transient status and error messages for operations such as loading lists, creating, updating, or deleting a list. It can also interpret optional URL parameters like status to show messages after redirects.
Handles navigation from each list in the overview to its detail page by constructing URLs to list_detail.html with the appropriate listId query parameter.
