component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader sits logically over the central content area rather than occupying its own permanent slot. When active, it overlays both the main column and the sidebar within the fixed-width central band, leaving the global header and nav visible above. A translucent, slightly grey-tinted layer covers the main content and sidebar, while a small loading indicator (spinner or text) is centered horizontally within the main column and placed slightly above the vertical midpoint of the viewport. The overlay respects the content width: it does not span edge-to-edge across the entire browser window, but aligns with the centered content wrapper, reinforcing the controlled, editorial layout. When inactive, it is not visually present and does not affect spacing between components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay fixed within the central content wrapper, positioned above both columns while active; does not scroll independently from the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-lists-overview component is the primary block in the main column and sits below the create form, separated from it by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the main column (about two-thirds of the centered content band), aligning its left edge with the main column’s text baseline and its right edge just before the gutter that separates it from the sidebar. Inside, individual reading lists are presented as a vertical stack of rows or cards, each occupying the full inner width, with 8–16px of vertical space between entries. The content is text-centric, so list titles and key metadata align flush-left, with no heavy visual framing, preserving the minimalist blog feel. The overall block extends downward as needed, becoming the dominant tall element on the page; the sidebar remains comparatively shorter, reinforcing this component’s visual weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow of the main column, expanding vertically as more lists are present and pushing the page to scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-create-form is positioned high in the main column to encourage creation, directly beneath the page title and the status banner (if present). It spans the full width of the main content column, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and a slightly larger top padding to echo the feel of a content block in the mockup. There is approximately 16–24px of whitespace separating it from the elements above and from the reading-lists-overview component below. Form fields are arranged in a simple vertical layout with labels aligned to the left and fields stretching to match the column’s inner width, maintaining short, readable line lengths. The overall height is compact, just enough to hold the input fields and a primary action button stacked with 8–16px spacing, avoiding visual heaviness while still feeling like a distinct, self-contained card within the editorial column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column’s vertical flow; when the form expands (e.g., validation messages), it pushes subsequent content downward while preserving the two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-edit-form appears contextually inside the main column, anchored directly to the list item being edited within the reading-lists-overview. When activated, it opens immediately below the corresponding list entry, sharing the same left and right alignment as the list item text so it visually feels like an extended detail of that row. It spans the full width of the main column and uses similar internal padding (16–24px) to the create form, but appears slightly inset vertically, with about 8–16px of space between the bottom of the list item and the top edge of the form to keep the relationship clear without feeling cramped. The form’s fields follow a vertical stacking pattern and are kept relatively compact to maintain the page’s low-to-medium density. As the edit form opens, it pushes all subsequent list items and content further down, preserving the clean, linear reading flow rather than overlaying other content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and contextual within the reading-lists-overview; appears and disappears within the normal flow, expanding the main column’s height where needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-delete-confirmation is presented as a modal dialog that floats above the entire central layout, similar in spirit to a classic confirmation box layered over a blog article. When active, a semi-transparent, slightly darkened overlay covers the full viewport, subtly dimming the header, main column, and sidebar while still allowing the underlying layout to be visible. The dialog itself is centered both horizontally and vertically in the viewport, with its width set to roughly 40–50% of the viewport on desktop, never exceeding the width of the central content band to keep it visually related to the page. Inside the dialog, content is stacked vertically: a clear title at the top, a brief confirmation message beneath it, and action buttons (confirm/cancel) arranged horizontally at the bottom right, all separated by 8–16px vertical spacing. The dialog has 24px or so of internal padding and a minimalist border or shadow, matching the understated aesthetic of the main layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal centered in the viewport, overlaying all other components while active and preventing interaction with the underlying content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-lists-status-banner sits near the top of the main column, directly below the page title and above the create form. It spans the full width of the main column, forming a low-height horizontal band that feels similar to a subtle notification bar within an article. The banner has modest internal padding (8–16px on all sides), a slightly tinted background (for success, error, or info) that contrasts gently yet clearly with the page background, and no heavy framing to keep the look clean. There is about 8–16px of vertical space separating it from the title above and the create form below. Text in the banner is left-aligned and brief, with any inline links styled in the accent color, acting as small visual anchors, consistent with the mockup’s treatment of links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column’s flow; appears and disappears without shifting the two-column structure, simply expanding or collapsing vertical space at the top of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-navigation-link group occupies the right sidebar column, mirroring the 'Archives' section from the blog mockup. It begins near the same vertical level as the top of the main column’s content—roughly aligned with the status banner and create form—creating a cohesive top edge between columns. The group is contained within a single sidebar block that spans the full width of the sidebar (about one-third of the central content band) and has 16–24px padding on all sides. At the top of this block, a simple heading such as 'Reading Lists' or 'Browse Lists' appears, followed by a vertical list of links to individual reading list overview or detail pages. Each link is left-aligned and occupies a single line, with 4–8px of vertical spacing between items, and is styled in the accent link color for clear but understated emphasis. The block’s total height is shorter than the main column’s content, so it occupies only the upper portion of the sidebar, allowing the main content to remain the dominant visual mass on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar; scrolls naturally with the page content and does not stick or float independently.

name: List Detail
title: List Detail
description: Displays the contents of a specific reading list or watchlist, with options to sort, filter, remove items, paginate, and open individual review detail pages.

core_features: Load and display the metadata of a specific reading list (name, type, description, total item count) using the listId URL parameter via getReadingListItems.
Show all reviews in the selected list (for the current page), including key fields such as title, content type, genre, rating, author name, thumbnail, and date added.
Allow users to remove individual reviews from the current reading list by calling removeReviewFromReadingList, with confirmation and feedback.
Allow users to open an individual review’s detail page by clicking its title, passing the reviewId, a from=list flag, and a returnUrl that points back to the current list.
Allow users to change the sort order within the list (for example by date added or rating) using the sortBy URL parameter, which is passed to getReadingListItems.
Support pagination within the list using page and pageSize URL parameters, which are passed to getReadingListItems and reflected in the pagination controls.
Provide optional simple client-side filtering within the loaded items (e.g., by content type or rating range) without additional API calls.
Provide navigation back to the overall Reading Lists page (reading_lists.html).
Handle loading, empty, and error states when retrieving list items.
user_workflows:
View a list:
1. User navigates to list_detail.html with a listId in the URL.
2. The page reads listId, sortBy, page, and pageSize from the URL.
3. The data-loader component calls getReadingListItems with these parameters.
4. On success, the list-header shows the list information and total count; the review-list component shows the current page of items; pagination-controls uses totalCount, page, and pageSize to render pagination.
5. If the list has no items, the empty-list-state component is displayed instead of the review-list.

Change sort order:
1. User opens the sort control in list-controls and selects a sort option.
2. list-controls updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets page to the first page.
3. The change in URL parameters triggers a new getReadingListItems call with the updated sortBy and page.
4. The review-list and pagination-controls re-render based on the new sorted results.

Change page size:
1. User selects a different page size in list-controls.
2. list-controls updates the pageSize parameter in the URL and resets page to the first page.
3. The data-loader calls getReadingListItems with the updated pageSize and page.
4. review-list and pagination-controls update to reflect the new number of items per page.

Paginate through the list:
1. User clicks a specific page number or a next/previous control in pagination-controls.
2. pagination-controls updates the page parameter in the URL, keeping listId, sortBy, and pageSize unchanged.
3. The URL change triggers getReadingListItems with the new page value.
4. The corresponding page of items is displayed in review-list.

Apply client-side filters within the current page:
1. User chooses filter criteria (e.g., content type, minimum rating) in list-controls.
2. list-controls updates its internal filter state (this does not call an API and does not change URL parameters).
3. review-list receives the filter settings as props and filters the current items array in memory before rendering.
4. User can clear filters to see the full set of items for the current page again.

Remove an item from the list:
1. User clicks the remove control on a specific item in review-list.
2. review-list triggers the remove-confirm-dialog with the selected reviewId and listId.
3. If the user confirms, remove-confirm-dialog calls removeReviewFromReadingList with listId and reviewId.
4. On success, remove-confirm-dialog notifies the data-loader (e.g., via an onRemoveSuccess event), which triggers a refresh by calling getReadingListItems with the current URL parameters.
5. If the refreshed page has no items but page is greater than the first page, data-loader may decrement page by one, update the URL, and call getReadingListItems again to show the previous page.
6. A success or error message is shown via notification-banner.

Open a review detail page and return to the list:
1. User clicks a review title in review-list.
2. review-list navigates to review_detail.html, passing id={reviewId}, from=list, and returnUrl set to the current list_detail URL (including listId and any current sort/pagination parameters).
3. On the review detail page, a Back action can use returnUrl to navigate back to the same list_detail view.
4. When the user returns, list_detail.html re-reads URL parameters and re-calls getReadingListItems to render the list state.

Go back to all reading lists:
1. User clicks the 'Back to all lists' control in list-header or empty-list-state.
2. The page navigates to reading_lists.html.
3. No API calls from this page are needed for that navigation.
interactions: Initial load: on page load, the data-loader component reads listId, sortBy, page, and pageSize from the URL and calls getReadingListItems. If listId is missing or invalid, an error is shown and the user can navigate back to reading_lists.html.
Sort selection: when the user changes the sort option, list-controls updates the sortBy URL parameter (and page), triggering a new getReadingListItems call and a re-render of the list.
Page size selection: when the user changes page size, list-controls updates the pageSize and page URL parameters, triggering a new getReadingListItems call.
Pagination click: when the user selects a new page, pagination-controls updates the page URL parameter, triggering a new getReadingListItems call.
Filter change: when the user adjusts filters in list-controls, the filter state is passed to review-list, which filters the current set of items in memory before rendering.
Remove item click: when the user clicks 'Remove' on an item, review-list opens remove-confirm-dialog for that reviewId and listId.

Confirm removal: when the user confirms in remove-confirm-dialog, removeReviewFromReadingList is called; on success, data-loader is instructed to refresh the current list via getReadingListItems; notification-banner displays success feedback.
Cancel removal: when the user cancels in remove-confirm-dialog, the dialog closes with no API call and no list changes.
Item title click: when the user clicks a review title, review-list navigates to review_detail.html with id, from=list, and a returnUrl to the current list_detail URL.
Back to all lists click: when the user clicks the back control in list-header or empty-list-state, the browser navigates to reading_lists.html.

Error handling: if getReadingListItems or removeReviewFromReadingList return an error, notification-banner displays an error message and the user can retry the action (e.g., via a 'Retry' button that re-calls getReadingListItems).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s main state is controlled via URL parameters and API responses. The required parameter is listId. Optional parameters are sortBy, page, and pageSize. On every load or URL change, data-loader reads these parameters and calls getReadingListItems({ listId, sortBy, page, pageSize }). The response determines the rendered state: list metadata (list object), current items (items array), and pagination data (totalCount, page, pageSize). sortBy defines the server-side ordering; changing sortBy updates the URL and triggers a new API call with page reset to the first page. page and pageSize define which slice of items is retrieved; changing either parameter updates the URL and triggers a new API call. After a successful removeReviewFromReadingList call, the list is reloaded using the current URL parameters. If the reloaded result has no items on a page greater than the first page, the page parameter is decremented, the URL is updated, and getReadingListItems is called again to fetch the previous page. Client-side filter settings are held in component state and applied only to the items returned by getReadingListItems; they do not affect URL parameters. Navigation to review_detail.html includes a returnUrl parameter built from the current list_detail URL (including listId, sortBy, page, and pageSize) so that returning to the list restores the same state through URL-based reloading.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads URL parameters (listId, sortBy, page, pageSize), calls getReadingListItems, maintains loading and error state, and distributes the resulting list, items, and pagination data to child components.
Displays the current list’s name, optional description, list type, and total item count; provides a 'Back to all lists' navigation control.

Provides controls to change sort order, page size, and in-memory filters (e.g., by content type or rating range). Updates URL parameters for sortBy and pageSize and exposes filter settings to the review-list component.

Renders the current page of list items returned by getReadingListItems, applies any local filters, and displays per-item information (title, content type, rating, genre, author name, thumbnail, date added). Provides clickable titles that navigate to review_detail.html and per-item remove controls that open the removal confirmation dialog.

Displays pagination information derived from totalCount, page, and pageSize and provides controls to navigate between pages. Updates the page URL parameter when the user changes pages, triggering re-fetch via data-loader.

Confirms the user’s intention to remove a review from the current list. When confirmed, calls removeReviewFromReadingList with listId and reviewId, then notifies the data-loader to refresh the list and triggers appropriate success or error notifications.
Shows transient success and error messages for operations such as loading list items and removing an item from the list.
Displays when getReadingListItems returns zero items for the list. Informs the user that the list has no items and provides a control to navigate back to reading_lists.html.

Displays when getReadingListItems fails due to an error (e.g., invalid listId or network issues), showing an error message and a retry option that re-calls getReadingListItems with the current URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width central content area that likely maps to a 12-column grid with an approximate 8/4 split between main content and sidebar. The content column appears centered with equal light-grey gutters on each side.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s classic blog pattern: a full-width site header at the very top, then a centered fixed-width content container that is split into a wider main column on the left and a narrower sidebar on the right. All List Detail–specific components live in the main column, stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order: notification banner (when present), list header, list controls, the review list (or its corresponding loading/empty/error state), and finally the pagination controls. Overlay components (data loader, confirmation dialog) appear above this stack without breaking the underlying vertical flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s classic blog pattern: a full-width site header at the very top, then a centered fixed-width content container that is split into a wider main column on the left and a narrower sidebar on the right. All List Detail–specific components live in the main column, stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order: notification banner (when present), list header, list controls, the review list (or its corresponding loading/empty/error state), and finally the pagination controls. Overlay components (data loader, confirmation dialog) appear above this stack without breaking the underlying vertical flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content container occupies roughly 70–75% of the viewport width, centered horizontally with light grey gutters on both sides. Within this container, the main column takes about two-thirds of the width (≈8 of 12 grid columns), while the right-hand sidebar uses the remaining third (≈4 of 12 columns). Each main-column component spans the full width of that column, with consistent internal padding of 16–24px and vertical gaps of 16–24px between sections. Overlays such as the confirmation dialog and data loader center in the viewport and temporarily sit above both columns, but the underlying main/side allocation remains visually clear.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a low-to-medium content density, mirroring an editorial reading experience. Text blocks, controls, and list items are given ample breathing room, with comfortable line lengths in the main column. Vertical spacing between sections is generous but not excessive (mostly 16–24px), and item lists (e.g., the review list) use 8–16px between rows to keep them readable but clearly separated. System feedback elements (banner, empty state, error state) use whitespace to feel calm and unobtrusive, rather than intrusive alerts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a strong vertical reading path with a slight F-pattern, adapted from the mockup. The eye moves from the site header to the top of the main column, first encountering the notification banner (if present), then the list title in the list header (the main anchor), drops to the list controls, and then flows down through the stack of review items. Occasional sideways glances to the right sidebar reflect the original F-pattern, but the list content remains dominant. Blue accent links and interactive controls punctuate this flow at the list controls area, within each review item, and in pagination, acting as subtle visual anchors. When modals or loading states appear, they temporarily take over the focal point by being centered and slightly elevated above the rest of the page.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar full-width blog header, the page content forms a centered, fixed-width band that occupies about three-quarters of the viewport, framed by soft grey gutters on the left and right. Inside this band, the layout splits into a classic two-column grid: a wider main column on the left and a narrower sidebar on the right, echoing a traditional editorial blog theme. All elements of the List Detail experience live in the main column, reinforcing it as the primary reading lane. At the very top of this column, a subtle notification banner (when present) stretches from left to right, followed by a prominent list header that feels analogous to a blog post title block. Just below, a horizontal strip of list controls spans the width of the main column, aligning dropdowns and filters into a single tidy row that invites interaction before scrolling. The bulk of the page is then occupied by a vertically stacked review list: rows of books or movies arranged in a clean, text-first manner with moderate spacing between items. At the bottom, centered or right-aligned pagination controls echo classic “Older/Newer posts” navigation, wrapping up the reading flow. Overlay states such as loading, empty, error, and the removal confirmation dialog appear centered over this structure, dimming or temporarily displacing the list while preserving the underlying vertical rhythm and the overall quiet, minimalist aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list data loader occupies the main content column in the exact space where the review list would normally appear. When active, it sits below the list controls and above the pagination area, spanning the full width of the main column (approximately two-thirds of the central content container). Vertically, it uses a generous block of space: the loading indicator (spinner or skeleton rows) is centered horizontally within the main column, with at least 48–64px of vertical whitespace above and below to maintain the calm, editorial feel. The rest of the page structure (header, sidebar, list header, list controls) remains visible, but the user’s eye is drawn to the loader as the primary focal point in the central reading lane.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the review-list content within the normal document flow. It does not scroll independently; instead, it scrolls with the page as part of the main column. Optionally, in an initial page-load state, it can be paired with a subtle translucent overlay limited to the main column to indicate non-interactivity of underlying list items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list header is the first List Detail–specific element in the main column, immediately below the global site header (and below the notification banner if one is visible). It is left-aligned with the main content column, spanning its full width (roughly two-thirds of the central content region). The list title is set in a prominent typographic style similar to a blog post title, occupying one to two lines, with 16–24px of top padding inside its block and 16–24px of bottom margin before the list controls. Any optional subtitle, description, or metadata (e.g., total items in the list) appears directly beneath the title, aligned to the same left edge, with 8–12px spacing, preserving the text-centric, editorial tone. There is ample whitespace above and below to clearly establish this as the primary identity of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the main column; it participates in normal vertical scrolling with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list controls sit directly below the list header, separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical space. They form a horizontal control bar spanning the full width of the main column. Controls such as sort dropdowns, filter chips, and view toggles are arranged on a single row: primary sorting options are left-aligned, while secondary filters or view-mode toggles can be right-aligned, creating a slight visual tension that matches the F-pattern scan (left to right, then down). The control bar has 12–16px of vertical padding, a subtle bottom border or faint background tint consistent with the minimalist style, and 16px of margin before the content area (review list or state). The overall density is moderate: controls are not cramped, with at least 8–12px horizontal spacing between elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main column’s vertical flow. It can optionally become sticky within the main column—when the user scrolls past its original position, it pins to the top edge of the viewport (just below the site header) while the review list scrolls beneath, ensuring that sorting and filtering remain accessible without disrupting the overall layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review list occupies the central portion of the page beneath the list controls, extending down the main column as a vertical stack of items. It begins roughly 16–24px below the list controls and spans the entire width of the main column (about two-thirds of the central content area). Each review item is presented as a horizontal row or light card, with a thumbnail or cover image on the left and text on the right, or as a purely text-based row if covers are omitted. Items are separated by 12–16px of vertical space and/or a subtle divider line, keeping the list airy but clearly segmented. The text within each row aligns with the left edge of the main column, maintaining a comfortable line length for reading. Actions such as “View review” and “Remove from list” are aligned consistently (typically right side within each item), using the accent color for links to match the mockup’s punctuating blue anchors. The list can extend well below the fold, with scrolling controlled by the page as a whole rather than by an internal scroll area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content within the main column. It yields space to the list-data-loader, empty-list-state, or list-loading-error-state: only one of these occupies this region at a time. When present, the review list pushes pagination-controls downward; when absent, states or loaders occupy its place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned at the bottom of the main column, directly beneath the review list (or aligned to the same vertical position when the list is present). There is a clear 24–32px gap above them to visually separate navigation from the list items. The pagination block spans the full width of the main column but centers its controls horizontally: a row of page numbers, previous/next links, or simple “Older / Newer” links arranged in a compact line. The spacing between pagination elements is comfortable (8–12px), and they use the accent color for active/hover states, echoing traditional blog pagination. If the list is very short, the pagination still sits close to the list, leaving ample whitespace below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level element at the end of the main column’s flow. It scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport. When empty-list-state or list-loading-error-state is active, pagination controls are hidden entirely to avoid distraction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove confirmation dialog appears centered both vertically and horizontally within the viewport, visually floating above the main content and sidebar. It is a compact rectangular panel, roughly 320–400px wide, with 24px padding on all sides, a simple title (“Remove from list?”) at the top, a short explanatory text block, and confirm/cancel buttons at the bottom right. The dialog maintains the site’s minimalist aesthetic: light background, subtle border or soft shadow, and clear typographic hierarchy. Behind it, the rest of the page (header, columns, list) is dimmed with a semi-transparent overlay, ensuring the dialog becomes the singular focal point without feeling heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Modal overlay fixed to the viewport, independent of page scroll. It covers both main and sidebar columns with a translucent backdrop that prevents interaction with underlying elements until the dialog is dismissed. The page can remain scrollable in the background or be temporarily locked, depending on implementation, but visually the dialog stays fixed in the center.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification banner sits directly beneath the global site header and above the list header, spanning the entire width of the central content container (crossing both main column and sidebar). It has a low-profile height with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding. The text is left-aligned, starting at the same left edge as the main column content, creating a clean alignment with the list header below. The banner uses the site’s accent color or a subtle tinted background to differentiate success, warning, or error states, while keeping typography calm and unobtrusive. When present, it nudges the rest of the content downward by its height, maintaining consistent vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element anchored near the top of the page’s content area. It appears and disappears in place without overlapping other elements, pushing the main column content down or allowing it to rise up when dismissed. It scrolls away with the page and is not sticky unless explicitly configured to be so.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty list state occupies the space where the review list would normally appear, beginning just below the list controls and spanning the full width of the main column. Rather than a dense block, it presents a light, centered message: a concise headline (e.g., “This list is empty”) and a short explanatory sentence, possibly accompanied by a small, simple icon. The content is horizontally centered within the main column, with large top and bottom margins (32–48px) to emphasize the state without feeling alarmist. The text is still left-aligned within its small central block to maintain the editorial reading feel. Below the main message, a primary call-to-action link or button (e.g., “Browse reviews” or “Add items”) is placed with about 16px of spacing. The overall effect is quiet and spacious, clearly communicating the empty state while preserving the core blog-like aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the review-list region in the main column and hides pagination-controls while active. It participates in the normal scroll and does not overlay other components. When shown, list-data-loader and list-loading-error-state are not displayed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list loading error state also sits in the main column, in the same vertical slot as the review list, directly below the list controls. It spans the full width of the main column but keeps its content constrained to a central text block, similar to the empty state. A brief error headline (e.g., “We couldn’t load this list”) is followed by a one- to two-line description and a clearly visible retry action (link or button) placed with 16px of spacing below the text. A subtle error icon or colored accent bar may appear to the left of the headline or along the top edge of the block, but the overall composition remains minimalist and text-centric. Extra vertical padding (24–32px top and bottom) ensures the state stands apart from surrounding content without feeling overwhelming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same region as the review-list and empty-list-state, pushing pagination-controls out of view while active. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay other components. Only one of list-data-loader, empty-list-state, list-loading-error-state, or review-list is visible in this region at any given time.

name: Author Profile
title: Author Profile
description: Displays a review author's profile with their bio, statistics, list of reviews, and the ability to follow or unfollow the author, with navigation to individual review detail pages.

core_features: Load and display author information (name, bio, avatar, reviewCount, averageRating, followersCount, isFollowed) using the getAuthorProfile interface based on the author ID in the URL.
Display a list of all reviews returned by getAuthorProfile for the author, including title, contentType, genre, rating, publishedAt, and thumbnailUrl.
Allow the user to follow or unfollow the author using the followAuthor interface, updating the isFollowed state and followersCount in the UI.
Enable navigation from each review in the list to the corresponding review_detail.html page, passing the review ID and context parameters (from=author, returnUrl=author.html?id={id}) via the URL.
Handle loading and error states for the author profile data when calling getAuthorProfile, including retry behavior without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
View an author's profile from a review detail page:
1) The user is on review_detail.html and activates a navigation element (such as the reviewer name) that links to author.html?id={authorId}.
2) The browser navigates to author.html with the id URL parameter set to the selected author's ID.
3) On page load, the page controller reads the id parameter from the URL and calls getAuthorProfile with authorId equal to this value.
4) While waiting for the response, a loading state is shown in place of the author profile and review list.
5) When getAuthorProfile returns successfully, the author header, stats summary, follow button, and review list components render the author and reviews data.
6) The user can scroll the page to read the bio and browse the list of reviews.

Follow or unfollow an author:
1) The user loads author.html?id={authorId}, triggering getAuthorProfile.
2) Once loaded, the follow button displays its state based on author.isFollowed from the API response.
3) To follow an author that is not yet followed, the user clicks the follow button.
4) The follow button component immediately sets a local pending state and calls followAuthor with parameters authorId and isFollowed set to the new desired state.
5) When followAuthor returns successfully, the component updates the displayed isFollowed state and followersCount using the values from the response.
6) If followAuthor returns an error, the button exits the pending state, shows an error message, and keeps the previous isFollowed state.
7) To unfollow, the user clicks the button again, which repeats the same flow with isFollowed toggled.

Navigate from an author profile to a specific review detail page:
1) The user is viewing author.html?id={authorId} and scrolls through the list of reviews loaded via getAuthorProfile.
2) The user clicks the title of a review in the list.
3) The review-list item constructs a URL for review_detail.html with parameters: id set to the selected review's reviewId, from set to "author", and returnUrl set to "author.html?id={authorId}" using the current page's author id.
4) The browser navigates to review_detail.html with these URL parameters.
5) From review_detail.html, the user can use browser navigation or the provided returnUrl to return to the same author profile view.

Handle missing or invalid author ID in the URL:
1) The user navigates to author.html without an id parameter or with an invalid id.
2) On page load, the page controller checks the presence and format of the id parameter.
3) If id is missing or invalid, the page does not call getAuthorProfile and instead displays an error state component with guidance to select a valid author (for example, by returning to a previous page).
4) If id is present but getAuthorProfile returns an error (such as an author not found condition), the page shows an error state with a retry option that re-calls getAuthorProfile using the same id when activated.
interactions: On page load (or when the id URL parameter changes), the page controller calls getAuthorProfile with authorId set to the id parameter and updates the loading, success, or error states of the page according to the API response.

Clicking the follow/unfollow button calls followAuthor with the current author's ID and the toggled isFollowed value, updates the UI based on the response, and temporarily disables the button while the request is in progress.

Clicking the title area of a review in the list triggers navigation to review_detail.html with URL parameters: id equal to that review's reviewId, from equal to "author", and returnUrl equal to the current author profile URL with its id parameter.

Optional interactions on the review list, such as clicking sort or filter controls, update only the client-side ordering or filtering of the reviews array returned by getAuthorProfile without making additional API calls.
Clicking a retry button in the error state component (for a failed getAuthorProfile request) re-invokes getAuthorProfile using the current id URL parameter.

If the browser's address bar URL changes to a different id while the page is loaded (e.g., via in-app navigation), the page controller detects the change and re-runs the workflow to call getAuthorProfile for the new authorId.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the id URL parameter, which represents the authorId of the profile to display. When author.html loads, or whenever the URL changes, the page controller reads the id parameter and, if present and valid, calls getAuthorProfile({ authorId: id }). While waiting for the response, a loading state flag is set and rendered by the loading component. If the API call succeeds, the returned author object and reviews array are stored in the page's in-memory state (not in localStorage) and are passed down to child components (header, stats, follow button, review list). If the call fails, an error state flag is set, and the error component is shown with a retry control that reuses the same id parameter. The author.isFollowed and followersCount fields from getAuthorProfile define the initial follow state. When the user interacts with the follow button, the component calls followAuthor({ authorId, isFollowed: desiredState }). While the followAuthor request is pending, the button uses a local pending flag to prevent duplicate submissions. After a successful response, the component updates the in-memory author object with the returned isFollowed and followersCount values so that all components bound to Author reflect the new state. The page does not encode follow state or any other interaction state into URL parameters; only the author identity (id) and outbound navigation parameters (from and returnUrl when linking to review_detail.html) are encoded in URLs. For outbound navigation, each review link sets from=author and returnUrl=author.html?id={id}, where {id} is the current author id from the URL, ensuring that the destination page can support returning to this profile view without using localStorage.

components.functionality: Initializes the author profile page by reading the id URL parameter, invoking getAuthorProfile with authorId equal to that id, managing loading and error states, and distributing the resulting author and reviews data to child components.

Encapsulates the call to getAuthorProfile, given an authorId supplied by the page controller, and normalizes the response into an author object and a reviews array for consumption by other components.
Displays the core author information from the Author model, including avatarUrl, name, and bio, and renders nested components such as the stats summary and follow button.
Shows key author statistics such as reviewCount, averageRating, and followersCount, updating automatically when the bound Author data changes (for example, after a followAuthor call).

Renders a button whose label and state are determined by author.isFollowed. When clicked, it toggles the follow state by calling followAuthor with the author's id and the desired isFollowed value, then updates the Author model's isFollowed and followersCount from the API response.

Receives the reviews array from getAuthorProfile and manages optional client-side sorting or filtering (e.g., by contentType or rating) before passing the resulting list to the author-review-list component.

Iterates over the processed reviews array and renders each entry via the author-review-list-item component, ensuring that each item knows the current author id for constructing navigation URLs to review_detail.html.

Displays individual review information such as title, contentType, genre, rating, publishedAt, and thumbnailUrl, and handles click events on the title to navigate to review_detail.html with URL parameters id (reviewId), from="author", and returnUrl set to the current author profile URL using the bound Author id.
Shows a loading indicator while getAuthorProfile is in progress, controlled by the page controller's loading flag, and hides itself once the Author and Review data are available or an error occurs.

Displays an error message and a retry control when getAuthorProfile fails or when the id URL parameter is missing or invalid. The retry control re-triggers the data loading flow using the current id from the URL by instructing the page controller to call getAuthorProfile again.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width central content area that likely maps to a 12-column grid with an approximate 8/4 split between main content and sidebar. The content column appears centered with equal light-grey gutters on each side.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same classic two-column blog template as the mockup: a full-width site header at the top, and beneath it a centered content area split into a wider main column on the left and a narrower sidebar on the right. All author profile content (header, stats, follow action, and review list) lives in the main left column, stacked vertically in a simple top-to-bottom flow. The right sidebar remains visually present but is handled by global layout, so this page’s components never break out of the main column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same classic two-column blog template as the mockup: a full-width site header at the top, and beneath it a centered content area split into a wider main column on the left and a narrower sidebar on the right. All author profile content (header, stats, follow action, and review list) lives in the main left column, stacked vertically in a simple top-to-bottom flow. The right sidebar remains visually present but is handled by global layout, so this page’s components never break out of the main column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content region is fixed-width and centered, occupying about 70–75% of the viewport width, with light grey gutters on both sides. Inside this, the main author column uses roughly 8 of 12 columns (~66% of the content region) and the sidebar uses the remaining ~34%. Within the main column, the author header and stats use the full column width, followed by a full-width reviews section. Vertical spacing follows the 8–24px rhythm: 32px between major blocks, 16–24px inside each section, and 8–12px between closely related items.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium, tuned for comfortable reading. Each section has generous padding (16–24px) and clear separation from the next. The author header and stats are compact but not cramped, with enough breathing room to feel editorial rather than dashboard-like. The review list is moderately dense—list items are clearly separated with white space between them but not oversized, echoing the blog-post list feel from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a vertical reading flow with a slight F-pattern. After scanning the global site title and top nav, attention moves to the author header at the top-left of the main column, then to the follow button on the right side of that same row. From there, it drops down to the stats summary, then continues vertically through the ‘Reviews by this author’ section heading and the list of reviews. The right sidebar remains a peripheral, secondary focus the eye may occasionally glance at, but the primary flow is down the main author column. Accent-colored links (review titles) act as visual anchors inside the list, similar to the blue links in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width site header and navigation, a fixed-width content area is centered on the page, leaving soft grey gutters on the left and right. This content band is divided into a wider main column on the left and a narrower sidebar on the right, matching a traditional blog layout. In the main column, the author profile begins near the top with the author’s name and basic identity information aligned to the left, while a follow/unfollow button sits to the right on the same horizontal line, giving the header a subtle two-part structure. Immediately below, a compact stats row or block presents key numbers about the author, then a clearly separated ‘Reviews by this author’ section introduces a vertical list of review items stretching down the page. Each review entry is presented as a text-focused row with a prominent linked title and supporting metadata, stacked one after another with consistent spacing. Loading and error states temporarily occupy the center of this main column when needed, replacing the content area while preserving the page’s overall structure. The right sidebar remains visually lighter and shorter, so the main author column feels dominant and continuous, reinforcing the reading-focused, editorial tone of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller is non-visual and sits conceptually around the entire author profile view. It governs what appears inside the main content column but does not render any visible box or area. Spatially, it corresponds to the full height of the main author content column, from just below the global site header to the bottom of the author’s review list, ensuring that loading or error states can temporarily replace this vertical stack of components without changing the page’s two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible structural wrapper that controls which child components are rendered within the main column; it follows the normal document flow and does not introduce any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is also non-visual and lives directly inside the main content area governed by the page controller. It occupies no explicit space on the screen but determines whether the loading state, error state, or the full stack of author header, stats, follow button, and reviews are shown. Functionally, it spans the vertical region where those components would appear, aligning with the main column’s left edge and width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible functional layer that switches content within the main column; it does not affect layout flow or introduce any fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The author header is the first visible component in the main content column, positioned approximately 32px below the global site header and nav. It aligns flush with the left edge of the main column and spans the full width of that column (about two-thirds of the centered content region, or roughly half of the overall viewport on a typical desktop). Internally, the header forms a subtle two-column row: on the left, the author’s name and short descriptor stack vertically with 4–8px spacing; on the right, space is reserved for the follow-author-button aligned to the top or vertical center, creating a balanced horizontal line. The block has 16–24px padding on all sides, with slightly more space (24px) at the top to visually separate it from the global header. The bottom of this header sits about 16px above the author-stats-summary, reinforcing a clear but tight grouping between the header and the stats beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the main scrollable flow; it stays fixed in place within the document and scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stats summary appears directly below the author header in the main column, maintaining the same left and right alignment and width as the header. It begins about 16px beneath the bottom edge of the header, signaling that it is closely related information. The stats are presented in a single horizontal row or a tight grid, occupying the full main column width but with each stat item separated by 16–24px of horizontal spacing. The stats block has 16px internal padding on all sides, with a simple, flat background to keep the look understated. Its vertical height is moderate—roughly the height of one to two lines of text plus padding—so it reads as a compact band of information between the header and the larger reviews section below. A larger vertical gap (24–32px) is left between the bottom of the stats summary and the top of the author-reviews-section to clearly delineate the transition from profile metadata to content list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally under the author header within the main column; scrolls with the rest of the content and has no special sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The follow/unfollow button is visually attached to the author-header and is placed on the right side of that header’s horizontal band. It aligns with the right edge of the main content column and sits horizontally opposite the author’s name, creating a subtle left-right tension that echoes the F-pattern scanning for primary actions. Vertically, the button is positioned either at the top alignment of the author name or vertically centered within the header block, depending on the available height, but it never extends beyond the padding of the header. The button itself is modest in size, approximately the width of 2–3 words of label with 12–16px internal padding, and it sits with at least 8–12px margin from any text or edge around it, maintaining the clean, airy feel. On narrow viewports, it can drop below the author name but remains within the same padded header block, preserving its strong association with the author identity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the author-header container; participates in the header’s normal flow and scrolls with the page. No fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reviews section begins below the stats summary in the main column, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space to mark it as a new major content area. It aligns with the same left and right boundaries as the header and stats, fully occupying the width of the main column. At the top of this section, a section heading such as ‘Reviews by this author’ is left-aligned and given 16–24px of space above the first review item. The section itself can be thought of as a vertical container that stretches down the page, with 16–24px of internal padding on the left and right and slightly more top padding to give the heading breathing room. The author-review-list lives inside this container, with the section’s bottom trailing off with the last review item and about 32px of margin before any subsequent page content or footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content block within the main column; it does not float or stick, but extends vertically as long as there are review items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review list is a vertical stack directly inside the author-reviews-section, starting immediately below the section heading with about 16px of spacing. It occupies the full width of the section, which in turn matches the main column width. Each author-review-list-item is placed one after another from top to bottom with consistent gaps of about 16–24px between items, echoing the blog-post listing style in the mockup. The list itself has no additional borders or background; it relies on the spacing and the section container’s padding to create structure. As the user scrolls, the list creates a continuous column of review entries that can extend far beyond the fold, while remaining visually lighter than the initial header area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A simple vertical list that extends as needed; it scrolls normally within the page and does not introduce any sticky or paginated behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each review list item is a horizontally full-width row within the main column, bounded by the author-reviews-section’s left and right padding. The top of an item starts about 16–24px below the previous item, creating clear separation without feeling sparse. Within the item, the review title is left-aligned at the top, using the accent link color to act as a visual anchor much like the blue links in the mockup. Beneath the title, short metadata (e.g., book/movie name, rating, date) is arranged in a single line or two compact lines with 4–8px of vertical spacing from the title. Optionally, a one–two-line summary excerpt sits below the metadata with another 8–12px gap, maintaining readability and an editorial feel. The item height flexes based on content but stays visually grounded by generous line height and consistent vertical padding (about 8–16px top and bottom inside the item’s content), with no heavy borders—just whitespace and subtle typographic hierarchy defining each card-like row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each item participates in the normal vertical stacking of the list; it does not float or reposition itself, and simply scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loading state occupies the same space as the main author content in the left column. It is positioned below the global site header, aligned to the left edge of the main column and spanning its full width. Vertically, any existing author header, stats, and review list are hidden and replaced with a simple centered loading indicator and optional brief message. The loader is centered horizontally within the main column and placed slightly below the top (roughly 64–96px from the top of the content area) to mirror the comfortable spacing of the header while still appearing near the top of the visible area. Surrounding whitespace remains generous, reinforcing the minimalist, unobtrusive feel while indicating that content is pending.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the main column’s content within the same scrollable area; does not overlay the header or become fixed, and scrolls away like normal content if the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state appears in the same main column location as the loading state and normal content, aligned to the left edge and spanning the full width of the main column. When triggered, it replaces the author header, stats, and review list with a concise error message and any retry action. The message block is centered horizontally within the main column and positioned near the top of the content area (around 64–96px below the global header), with ample white space above and below to match the clean, editorial style. The text is left-aligned for readability, with 16–24px of internal padding and at least 16px of spacing between the message and any action buttons, keeping the layout calm and uncluttered even in error conditions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the main column content within the existing flow; it scrolls normally and does not overlay or fix itself to the viewport.

name: Newsletter
title: Newsletter Preferences
description: Configure newsletter subscription details, including email, frequency, category-specific schedules, and rating filters for book and movie reviews.

core_features: Load existing newsletter subscription settings using getNewsletterSubscription and prefill the form when available.
Allow entering or updating subscriber name and email address as the basis for the subscription.
Provide a main frequency control (none, daily, weekly, monthly, custom_per_category) that governs how often newsletters are sent.
When mainFrequency is custom_per_category, enable separate frequency controls for booksFrequency and moviesFrequency.
Offer a toggle to only receive reviews rated 4 stars and above, mapping to sendOnlyHighRated and minRating in the subscription.
Validate and submit newsletter preferences via saveNewsletterSubscription, handling both creation and update flows.
Reflect the latest saved subscription state on the page and optionally in the URL via status parameters (e.g., after successful save).
user_workflows:
New subscription setup:
1. User navigates to newsletter.html (direct link or footer navigation).
2. Page initialization calls getNewsletterSubscription.
3. If no subscription is returned, the form is shown empty (except any prefill from URL parameters like email or name).
4. User enters email and optionally name.
5. User selects a mainFrequency value.
6. If mainFrequency is custom_per_category, user sets booksFrequency and moviesFrequency.
7. User optionally enables the high-rating-only toggle, which sets sendOnlyHighRated and a minRating of 4.
8. User submits the form.
9. Page validates required fields and calls saveNewsletterSubscription with the current form values.
10. On success, the page updates the displayed state from the returned subscription and may update the URL to include status=saved.

Update existing subscription:
1. User visits newsletter.html.
2. Page calls getNewsletterSubscription and receives an existing subscription.
3. The form is prefilled with email, name, mainFrequency, booksFrequency, moviesFrequency, sendOnlyHighRated, and minRating from the subscription.
4. The primary action label is set to indicate updating preferences rather than initial subscription.
5. User adjusts any combination of name, email, frequency settings, or rating filter.
6. User submits the form.
7. The page validates the changes and calls saveNewsletterSubscription.
8. On success, the local form state is updated to reflect the latest subscription and a confirmation state is shown (driven by returned success and optionally status URL parameter).

Disable or reduce newsletter frequency:
1. User loads newsletter.html and existing settings are prefilled via getNewsletterSubscription.
2. User changes mainFrequency to none to effectively stop newsletters, or selects a less frequent option (e.g., monthly).
3. For mainFrequency set to custom_per_category, user can independently set booksFrequency or moviesFrequency to none.
4. User submits the form.
5. The system calls saveNewsletterSubscription with the updated frequency values.
6. On success, the page reflects the new effective frequency and any isActive change returned in the subscription.

Adjust rating filter for newsletters:
1. User loads newsletter.html and sees current sendOnlyHighRated and minRating state.
2. To receive only higher-rated content, user enables the high-rating-only toggle; this sets sendOnlyHighRated to true and minRating to 4 if not already set.
3. To receive all reviews regardless of rating, user disables the high-rating-only toggle; this sets sendOnlyHighRated to false and clears or ignores minRating on save.
4. User submits the form.
5. The page validates the combination (e.g., minRating present when sendOnlyHighRated is true) and calls saveNewsletterSubscription.
6. On success, the updated filter state is confirmed on-screen.

Accessing the page with prefilled values or return status via URL:
1. Another part of the site links to newsletter.html with optional query parameters such as email, name, or mainFrequency.
2. On load, the page reads URL parameters and calls getNewsletterSubscription.
3. If a subscription exists, URL-provided values are used only where there is no existing value (e.g., to prefill email if subscription.email is empty).
4. If no subscription exists, URL parameters fully prefill the initial form state.
5. After a successful save, the page may update the URL to include a status parameter (e.g., status=saved) so that a refresh or return to the page shows a persisted confirmation message driven by the URL state.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- Event: onInit (page load).
- Action: Call getNewsletterSubscription.
- Behavior: If subscription is returned, populate all bound form fields. If not, initialize with empty values and optionally overlay URL-passed email, name, or mainFrequency when present.

Name input:
- Interaction: User types in the name field.
- Event: onChange (name input).
- Behavior: Update in-memory subscription form state for name; no API call until form submission.
- Validation: Optional field; no blocking validation, but may trim whitespace on blur.

Email input:
- Interaction: User types in the email field.
- Events: onChange (update state), onBlur (validate format).
- Behavior: Update form state for email.
- Validation: Required; on submit, if email is empty or invalid, prevent submission and show validation error linked to this field.

Main frequency selection:
- Interaction: User selects a mainFrequency value from a set of options derived from the NewsletterSubscription model values.
- Event: onChange (mainFrequency control).
- Behavior: Update form state mainFrequency. If the selected value is custom_per_category, enable category frequency controls. If the selected value is not custom_per_category, disable category frequency controls and either clear or ignore booksFrequency and moviesFrequency for submission.

Category-specific frequency selection:
- Interaction: When mainFrequency is custom_per_category, user selects values for booksFrequency and moviesFrequency.
- Event: onChange (per category frequency controls).
- Behavior: Update form state booksFrequency and moviesFrequency. When mainFrequency changes away from custom_per_category, these fields are treated as inactive and not submitted or are submitted in a neutral way as allowed by the API.

High-rating-only toggle:
- Interaction: User clicks a checkbox or similar control labeled to only send reviews rated 4+ stars.
- Event: onChange (rating filter control).
- Behavior when enabled: Set sendOnlyHighRated to true in form state and ensure minRating is set to 4 if minRating is not already compatible.
- Behavior when disabled: Set sendOnlyHighRated to false and clear or ignore minRating on submission.

Submit preferences:
- Interaction: User clicks the primary submit button (label may vary based on whether a subscription exists).
- Event: onClick or onSubmit (form submission).
- Behavior:
  1. Run client-side validation for required fields (email and mainFrequency; booksFrequency and moviesFrequency when mainFrequency is custom_per_category, and minRating when sendOnlyHighRated is true).
  2. If validation passes, call saveNewsletterSubscription with the current form state: email, name, mainFrequency, booksFrequency (if applicable), moviesFrequency (if applicable), sendOnlyHighRated, and minRating (if applicable).
  3. While waiting, disable the submit button and show a pending state.
  4. On success: read success, message, and subscription from the response, update local state with the returned subscription, re-enable controls, and show a confirmation message. Optionally, update the URL with a status parameter (e.g., status=saved) using navigation logic.
  5. On error (success=false): display the returned message as an error, keep form values intact for correction, and re-enable the button.

Status and confirmation messages:
- Interaction: Page reads URL parameter status on load or when the URL changes.
- Event: onInit and onURLChange.
- Behavior: When status indicates a previously successful save or another state (e.g., status=saved or status=error), display a corresponding informational or error message. Allow users to dismiss this message with a click, which may clear the status from local state and optionally from the URL.

Navigation interactions:
- Footer navigation: User clicks a 'Newsletter' link in the site footer, which navigates to newsletter.html (optionally with URL parameters such as mainFrequency preset). The page then runs its normal initialization flow.
- Direct access: User enters or follows newsletter.html in the browser. The same initialization and data load logic runs.
page_functionality.state_logic:
Page state is driven by the combination of API data and URL parameters, with no reliance on localStorage.

URL Parameters:
- email: Optional. If present on load, used to prefill the email field when getNewsletterSubscription returns no subscription or a subscription without an email value.
- name: Optional. If present on load, used to prefill the name field similarly to email.
- mainFrequency: Optional. If present on load, and if there is no existing subscription or the subscription mainFrequency is unset, it preselects the mainFrequency control with a value consistent with the NewsletterSubscription.mainFrequency enum.
- status: Optional. Used to control the visibility and type of a status message block. For example, status=saved can be used after a redirect or reload to show a persistent 'preferences saved' message.

Precedence Logic:
1. On page init, the page reads URL parameters and stores them in a temporary state object.
2. The page calls getNewsletterSubscription.
3. If a subscription is returned, its values are the primary source for form initialization. URL parameters only fill in fields that are missing or empty in the subscription record so that existing user choices are not overridden unintentionally.
4. If no subscription is returned, URL parameters are used to initialize form fields; any fields not present in the URL are initialized as empty or neutral according to the model.
5. The status parameter, if present, is used regardless of subscription existence to determine whether to show a success or informational message on load.

Runtime State Transitions:
- When the user modifies any form field, the changes are applied immediately to an in-memory representation of the pending subscription state.
- When the user changes mainFrequency away from custom_per_category, the UI state hides or disables category-specific controls, and the form submission logic either omits booksFrequency and moviesFrequency or submits them in an appropriate neutral form; these values are still preserved in memory so that returning to custom_per_category during the same session can restore them.
- When sendOnlyHighRated is toggled on, minRating is set or updated in in-memory state to 4, consistent with the high-rating requirement. When toggled off, minRating is cleared or ignored for save calls.
- After a successful save via saveNewsletterSubscription, the returned subscription object replaces the in-memory state so that subsequent edits start from the latest saved setup. The page may also update the URL (e.g., adding or updating status=saved) without a full reload so that the success state can persist across refreshes.
- If the user dismisses a status message, this updates in-memory UI state; optionally, navigation logic may modify the URL to remove the status parameter so the message does not reappear on reload.

components.functionality: Initializes the Newsletter page by reading URL parameters, calling getNewsletterSubscription, and constructing the initial in-memory subscription form state.
Manages the subscriber identification fields (name and email), including input state, basic validation, and integration with the overall newsletter form.
Controls the mainFrequency selection for the newsletter subscription, including enabling or disabling category-specific frequency controls based on whether custom_per_category is selected.
Provides separate frequency selectors for booksFrequency and moviesFrequency that are active only when mainFrequency is custom_per_category.

Manages the option to receive only reviews with ratings of 4 stars and above by binding to sendOnlyHighRated and minRating, and enforcing that minRating is set appropriately when the toggle is enabled.

Validates the newsletter form before submission, ensuring that required fields (email, mainFrequency, and when applicable booksFrequency, moviesFrequency, and minRating) are present and consistent with the chosen configuration.
Provides the primary submit button (Subscribe/Save Preferences) and coordinates the submission process, including validation and calling saveNewsletterSubscription.

Executes saveNewsletterSubscription with the current form state, processes the response, updates the in-memory subscription representation, and coordinates UI feedback such as success or error messages and URL status updates.
Displays informational, success, or error messages based on URL status parameters and the results of saveNewsletterSubscription, and allows the user to dismiss these messages.

component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background behavior; logically bound to the main newsletter column container and executes on initial page load without altering layout flow.
