It includes a brief status message aligned to the left, with any action link (e.g., 'Unsubscribe', 'Manage email') placed inline to the right of the message or aligned to the far right, respecting the single-line or two-line height. There is 16–24px of vertical space above the next component (subscriber-info-form), maintaining the spacious editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in normal document flow; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscriber-info-form is the first main form section in the newsletter column, positioned immediately below the subscription-status-banner with approximately 24px of vertical space separating them. It occupies the full width of the main content column and is rendered as a rectangular section with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. A simple section heading (e.g., 'Subscriber details') aligns left at the top of this block, separated from the first input field by 8–12px. Inside, the email address field and any name or identifier fields stack vertically, each stretching to about 90–100% of the section’s width (respecting internal padding). Labels are left-aligned directly above each input, with 4–8px spacing, and inputs have 8–12px vertical spacing between them. Helper text, if any, sits directly beneath the related input in a smaller, lighter type. The bottom of this section is separated from the next section (frequency-selector) by about 24px of white space, clearly delineating it as a distinct group.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main column; resizes responsively with the main column’s width but does not change its relative position in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The frequency-selector section appears directly below the subscriber-info-form, with roughly 24px of vertical spacing between the two blocks. It also spans the full width of the main column and uses similar 16–24px internal padding, creating a cohesive series of content blocks. A left-aligned heading such as 'Overall newsletter frequency' appears at the top of the section, with 8–12px of space above the first control. The frequency options (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly) are arranged in a single column for clarity, using either a vertical radio-button list or a dropdown. Each option is separated by 8px vertically, keeping density moderate. Any brief description (e.g., 'You’ll receive a combined digest with both books and movies') is placed just below the selector, aligned to the left, and constrained to the column width to match the editorial text style. The bottom of this section is again followed by about 24px of white space before the category-frequency-controls block starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in vertical flow, following the main column width and maintaining its position between subscriber-info-form and category-frequency-controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-frequency-controls section sits beneath the frequency-selector, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It spans the same full width of the main column, with 16–24px padding, and visually reads as a slightly more detailed editorial segment. A heading like 'Category-specific schedules' is left-aligned at the top, followed by a short explanatory line (optional) in smaller, lighter text, spaced 8–12px below the heading. Within the section, controls for 'Book reviews' and 'Movie reviews' stack vertically, each treated as a sub-row. Each sub-row begins with a category label on the left and its frequency control (e.g., dropdown, radio group, or toggle + dropdown) directly beneath or slightly indented below the label in the same column, keeping everything in a single-column flow to preserve readability. Vertical spacing of 12–16px separates the sub-rows so they feel clearly distinct without being bulky. Any per-category helper text is placed directly under its respective control. The section ends with a clean bottom edge and about 24px of space before the rating-filter-toggle section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section that grows vertically based on the amount of category controls but always occupies the full width of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating-filter-toggle section appears next in the vertical stack, approximately 24px below the category-frequency-controls. It shares the same full-width, padded block style to maintain visual continuity. At the top, a left-aligned heading such as 'Only include higher-rated reviews' introduces the section, followed by a concise descriptive sentence 8–12px below it that explains how the filter affects content. The primary control—a toggle or checkbox to enable filtering—is placed on the left near the top of the section’s interactive area, with its label to the right in a single line where possible. When the filter is enabled, additional rating threshold controls (e.g., a row of selectable star ratings or a dropdown like '4 stars and up') appear directly beneath the toggle, aligned with the left edge of the text, and spaced 8–12px below it. These controls are arranged vertically rather than side-by-side to respect the moderate line length and maintain the low-to-medium density. At the bottom, around 24px of vertical space separates this section from the newsletter-submit-controls, signifying the transition from configuration to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section, appearing near the bottom of the form; its height is conditional on whether extra controls are shown but it remains fully in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-submit-controls area anchors the bottom of the newsletter form in the main column. It sits approximately 24px below the rating-filter-toggle section and spans the full width of the main column, but uses slightly less vertical padding (around 16px) to feel compact and action-oriented. The primary action button (e.g., 'Save newsletter settings') is aligned to the left edge of the main column content, not centered, echoing the understated editorial style and the left-aligned text pattern. The button uses the site’s accent color, making it a strong visual focal point while still fitting into the minimalist palette. To the right of the primary button, separated by about 12–16px, appears a secondary action such as a link-style 'Cancel' or 'Reset to defaults', displayed in standard link color with no heavy button treatment. If an inline validation or success message is associated with submission, it appears just above the buttons, left-aligned, with 8–12px spacing from the buttons, and uses subtle coloring (e.g., soft green or red) without heavy borders. Below this control area, there is a slightly larger margin (24–32px) before the end of the main column content or any footer, providing a visually clear endpoint to the interactive portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level action row fixed at the bottom of the form content; scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This validation-logic component is non-visual and conceptually wraps the entire newsletter form in the main column, applying rules to subscriber-info-form, frequency-selector, category-frequency-controls, and rating-filter-toggle. While it doesn’t occupy any explicit space, its effects are seen through inline error messages and field states within those visible sections. Error text would appear directly under the relevant input, in a small, red-tinted type, with 4–8px spacing from the field, without significantly altering the overall section spacing. Section-level validation summaries would appear near the top of a section in place, adding minor vertical height but preserving the 16–24px rhythm between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller bound to fields across multiple sections; influences inline layout only when errors are present but otherwise has no dedicated spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-save-handler is an API-handler with no direct visual representation. It is associated with the newsletter-submit-controls and, by extension, the entire form contained in the main column. When the user triggers the primary submit action, this handler executes behind the scenes. Any loading or feedback states it triggers (such as disabling the submit button or showing a transient message near the status banner or inline above the buttons) are surfaced through existing components rather than a separate visual block. Thus, it occupies no dedicated space and does not modify the structural layout or spacing on its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background behavior; tied to the submit action within the newsletter-submit-controls without creating any new visual element in the layout.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for sending questions, feedback, or comments about specific reviews to the site owner via a contact form.

core_features: Display a contact form with required fields for name, email, and message, with basic client-side validation (non-empty fields, email format check) before submission.

Allow users to reference specific reviews in their message by typing or pasting review titles manually, and by optionally pre-filling context from URL parameters (for example, a review title passed from a review detail page).
Submit the contact form using the createContactMessage interface with the current name, email, and message values.
Show submission status feedback (success or error) based on the createContactMessage response, and optionally reflect the status in the URL so that the state can be restored on page reload.
Prevent duplicate submissions while a request is in progress by disabling the submit button until the createContactMessage call completes.
user_workflows:
Generic contact message:
1. User navigates to contact.html from the site footer or via direct URL without additional parameters.
2. Page loads with empty name, email, and message fields.
3. User types their name into the name field.
4. User types their email into the email field; the form validates the format on blur or before submission.
5. User types their message in the message textarea, optionally including any review title they want to reference.
6. User clicks the 'Send Message' button.
7. The contact form validates required fields and email format; if validation fails, inline errors are shown and submission is blocked.
8. If validation passes, the form calls createContactMessage with the current name, email, and message values and disables the submit button while waiting.
9. When a response is returned, if success is true, the form shows a success status message using the returned messageText, may reset the form fields, and updates the URL to include a status parameter indicating success. If success is false, an error status message is shown using messageText and the form remains filled so the user can adjust and retry.

Contact about a specific review via deep link:
1. User is reading a specific review on another page that provides a link to contact.html with URL parameters such as reviewTitle and optionally reviewSlug or reviewId.
2. User clicks that link and arrives at contact.html with the relevant URL parameters.
3. The contact page reads the URL parameters on load. If a reviewTitle parameter is present, a context panel displays the referenced review title and provides an action to insert this reference into the message.
4. The message field may be pre-populated with a template string that includes the reviewTitle, or the user can click an 'Insert into message' action which appends or inserts the reviewTitle into the current message cursor position.
5. User fills in name and email if not already filled, optionally editing the pre-populated message.
6. User clicks the 'Send Message' button, triggering the same validation and submission flow via createContactMessage as in the generic workflow.
7. On success, the page shows a success status (and optionally sets status=success in the URL). On error, an error status is shown, preserving the context and form data.

Handling validation and submission errors:
1. User begins filling the form but leaves one or more required fields empty or enters an invalid email format.
2. When the user blurs a field or clicks 'Send Message', the form validates each field.
3. If any validation errors are detected, the relevant field(s) show error messages and the submit action is blocked; no call to createContactMessage is made.
4. User corrects the invalid entries and clicks 'Send Message' again.
5. If createContactMessage returns success=false (for example, due to server-side validation or other issues), the status banner displays the returned messageText as an error description.
6. The form remains editable so the user can adjust the content and resubmit, repeating this workflow until success=true is returned or the user leaves the page.
interactions: Typing in form fields: As the user types in the name, email, and message inputs, the component updates its internal state and clears any existing error messages for that field.

Field blur validation: When a user leaves (blurs) the name or email field, the form checks if the field is non-empty; for the email field, it also checks that the value matches a basic email pattern and sets or clears validation errors accordingly.

Click 'Insert into message' (review reference): If URL parameters provide review context, the user can click an action control in the context panel that triggers an event to update the message field in the form, inserting or appending the referenced review title or other context text.

Click 'Send Message': When the 'Send Message' button is clicked, the form runs client-side validation on name, email, and message. If validation passes, it triggers the onSubmit handler, which:
- Sets a submissionInProgress state flag.
- Disables the 'Send Message' button and any duplicate-submit triggers.
- Calls createContactMessage with the current name, email, and message values.

Handling createContactMessage success: When the createContactMessage call resolves with success=true, the onSubmitSuccess handler:
- Displays a success status banner using the returned messageText.
- Optionally clears or retains the form fields based on the chosen UX (defined in implementation).
- Updates the URL to include a status=success parameter (and optionally a messageId parameter derived from contactMessage.id) so that the success state can be recognized on page reload.
- Resets submissionInProgress so the button becomes active again if needed.

Handling createContactMessage failure: When the createContactMessage call resolves with success=false, the onSubmitError handler:
- Displays an error status banner using the returned messageText.
- Leaves the current form values intact for editing.
- Ensures submissionInProgress is set to false to re-enable the submit button.
- Does not modify URL status parameters, or may set status=error if needed for state restoration.

Dismissing status messages: If the status banner includes a dismiss control, clicking it triggers an onClick handler that hides the banner and optionally removes any status parameter from the URL via a client-side navigation update.

Navigation from other pages: When the user clicks a 'Contact' link in the site footer, the browser navigates to contact.html without additional parameters. When navigation includes parameters (such as from a review detail page), those parameters are handled as described in the state logic.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Contact page uses URL parameters to manage both review context and submission status, without relying on localStorage. On page load, the component reads the current URLSearchParams. Recognized parameters include (names are implementation choices, not fixed values):
- reviewTitle: If present, the page treats this as the human-readable title of the review the user is contacting about. The review-context-panel uses this value to display context and can pass it into the contact-form so that the message field can be initialized or updated with a reference to this title. This does not invoke any backend interfaces; it is purely client-side.
- reviewSlug or reviewId: If present, these are handled purely as additional context for the user. The contact page may display them in the context panel or use them to construct a suggestion text that the user can insert into the message. No data is fetched from other interfaces, and no direct use of the Review model occurs on this page.
- messageTemplate or similar: If present, the page can pre-fill the message field or part of it with this text, allowing other pages to predefine message snippets (e.g., a generic feedback template) without storing them locally.
- status: After a successful submission, the page may update the URL to include a status parameter (for example, status=success or status=error). On subsequent loads or refreshes, the page reads this parameter to decide whether to show a persistent success or error banner without re-running createContactMessage. This allows the success state to survive a full page reload.
- messageId: Optionally, after a successful createContactMessage call, the page may add a messageId parameter using the returned contactMessage.id. While this page does not call any interfaces using this id, it can serve as a passive reference or for future navigation patterns.
Internal state such as the current values of name, email, message, form-level validation errors, and submissionInProgress are kept in component state and are not encoded into the URL. URL parameters are read once on load (and optionally on URL changes) to seed initial state (for example, pre-filling the message with review context) and to determine whether to show submission status banners. All backend interactions are restricted to the createContactMessage interface; no other model-specific interfaces or data fetching are used on this page.

components.functionality: Collects user input for name, email, and message, validates required fields and email format, and submits the data via the createContactMessage interface. Handles submission state (in progress, success, error) and coordinates with the status banner component.

Reads URL parameters related to a specific review (such as reviewTitle, reviewSlug, or reviewId) and presents this information as optional context above or beside the contact form. Provides controls that allow the user to insert or append this context into the contact-form message field, ensuring that the user can easily reference the exact review in their message.

Displays feedback messages about the outcome of form submissions, using the success boolean and messageText returned from createContactMessage, and optionally using the status URL parameter to restore status on reload. Shows success messages when submissions complete successfully and error messages when they fail, and allows the user to dismiss the banner.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width central content area that likely maps to a 12-column grid with an approximate 8/4 split between main content and sidebar. The content column appears centered with equal light-grey gutters on each side.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same two-column editorial layout as the mockup: a full-width header at the top, then a centered content band with a left main column and a right sidebar. In the right sidebar, the review-context-panel sits as a self-contained block near the top, mirroring how the Author/Archives sections are stacked in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same two-column editorial layout as the mockup: a full-width header at the top, then a centered content band with a left main column and a right sidebar. In the main column, the Contact page starts with a page title, followed by a short introductory sentence, then the contact-status-banner (when present), and finally the vertically stacked contact-form fields. In the right sidebar, the review-context-panel sits as a self-contained block near the top, mirroring how the Author/Archives sections are stacked in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a fixed-width central content area occupying about 70–75% of the viewport width, centered with light grey gutters on each side. Inside this, allocate roughly two-thirds of the width to the main column (where the contact form lives) and one-third to the sidebar (for the review-context panel). Vertically, reserve a modest band below the header for the page title and intro, then allow the form to dominate the scrollable area, with the sidebar block remaining visually lighter and shorter, similar to the original blog layout.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep a comfortable, text-centric density. Form fields and labels should be in a single vertical flow with 16–24px spacing between field groups and 8–16px between label and input, echoing the spacious editorial feel. The review-context-panel should be a compact but readable text block with clear line spacing, similar to the Author and Archives sections: readable but not crowded. The status banner should have enough padding (around 16–24px) to feel distinct without overwhelming surrounding content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F-pattern flow: the user’s eye moves from the header (logo and navigation) across the top, then drops down to the Contact page title in the main column. Immediately below, the status banner (when visible) becomes a quick focal point, after which the eye continues down through the form fields. Peripheral glances to the right pick up the review-context-panel in the sidebar, but the main vertical reading path remains anchored on the left. Blue or accent-colored links in the context panel and within the form help punctuate this flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width header with the site title on the left and navigation on the right, the Contact page content sits in a centered, fixed-width band that leaves generous light-grey gutters on both sides. This band is split into a wider left column and a narrower right sidebar, echoing a classic blog reading layout. At the top of the left column, the word “Contact” appears as a simple, prominent page title, followed closely by a one- or two-line explanatory sentence. Directly beneath this, when relevant, a status banner stretches the full width of the main column, using the site’s accent color and clear text to indicate success or error states. Below that, the contact form flows downward as a stack of full-width fields with clear labels and ample vertical spacing, forming the dominant visual area on the page. On the right, the review-context-panel sits near the top of the sidebar as a slim card-like block with a concise heading and short descriptive text or links about the specific review being referenced. The overall impression is clean, balanced, and text-first: the main column invites focused interaction with the form, while the sidebar provides gentle contextual support without competing for attention.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form occupies the primary main column on the left side of the content area, directly beneath the page title, intro text, and the status banner (when present). It spans nearly the full width of this column, leaving only a modest 16–24px inner margin on the left and right so that form fields align visually with the page title and header content above. The form is arranged as a vertical stack of elements: each field group (label plus input or textarea) runs full width within the main column, with labels placed above their corresponding fields. Between each field group, leave approximately 16–24px vertical spacing to maintain a relaxed, editorial feel rather than a dense, app-like form. The top edge of the form starts about 24px below the status banner (or the intro text if the banner is absent), creating a clear but not exaggerated separation. The submit button is left-aligned with the fields and sits 24px below the last input, with a slightly larger top margin to visually signal the end of the form. Overall, the form’s height will exceed the sidebar in most cases, reinforcing that this column is the main interactive area and guiding the user’s scroll down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the main content flow; it scrolls with the page and always remains aligned to the left main column in a responsive but fixed-width center layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review-context-panel sits in the right-hand sidebar, starting near the top of that column and visually aligned with the top of the contact form’s header area on the left. It occupies roughly one-third of the total content width, matching the sidebar proportion from the mockup. The panel is presented as a single, self-contained content block with a clear heading (e.g., “About this review” or “Context”) at the top, followed by a short paragraph or list of links that reference the relevant review or reviews. Internally, the panel has uniform padding of about 16–24px on all sides, with the heading separated from the body copy by 8–16px. Below the panel, leave at least 24px of vertical space before any additional sidebar content (if added later), mirroring the stacked sections seen in the Author/Archives area of the original layout. The panel’s overall height is moderate: tall enough for comfortable reading but clearly shorter than the main contact form column, helping it feel like supportive context rather than a competing primary feature.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky sidebar block that scrolls naturally with the page. It stays fixed within the right column and does not overlay or float over the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-status-banner appears within the main left column, directly below the Contact page title and introductory text, and directly above the contact form. It spans the full width of the main column, aligning with the left and right edges of the form fields and title to create a clean vertical alignment. The banner has a visually distinct background using the site’s accent color (or a tinted variant), with approximately 16–24px of padding on all sides, giving it enough presence to be noticed without overwhelming the page. Text within the banner is left-aligned and limited to one or two concise lines, preserving the minimalist, editorial feel. There is about 16–24px vertical spacing above the banner (to separate it from the intro text) and another 24px below (to separate it from the top of the form), so it reads as a discrete, temporary message between the page header and the interactive area. When not needed, the banner collapses entirely, allowing the form to move up and sit closer to the intro text. Its position does not change with scrolling; instead, users encounter it at the top of the content when they first return to the page after submitting the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline notification within the main column; it appears and disappears in the normal document flow without fixed or sticky behavior, pushing the form content down when visible.

name: Account & Preferences
title: Account & Preferences
description: Page for creating the single-user account, viewing basic profile details, and managing default personal preferences such as newsletter opt-in.

core_features: Detect whether a single-user account already exists by calling getAccountProfile on page load and display either account details/preferences or the account creation form accordingly.
Provide a 'Create Account' form with username, email, and password fields that calls createAccount on submit and then refreshes the displayed account state.
Display read-only account information (e.g., username, email, account creation date, current default newsletter opt-in) returned from getAccountProfile or createAccount.
Expose a basic preferences form for updating default newsletter opt-in using updateAccountPreferences and immediately reflect changes in the displayed account information.
Handle success and error feedback messages for createAccount and updateAccountPreferences actions and keep them in sync with URL-based state (e.g., which section is active).
user_workflows:
Workflow: First-time visitor creating an account
1) User navigates to account.html (optionally via the 'Sign Up' header link).
2) The page reads URL parameters. If none are provided or view=create, the page attempts to determine state by calling getAccountProfile.
3) If getAccountProfile returns no existing account, the page sets mode=create and shows the Create Account form.
4) User fills in username, email, and password and submits the form.
5) The createAccount interface is called with the submitted values.
6) If createAccount returns success=true, the page updates internal account state with the returned account object, updates the URL to account.html?view=profile, and hides the Create Account form.
7) The account overview and preferences sections become visible, pre-populated from the returned account data, including defaultNewsletterOptIn.
8) If createAccount returns success=false, the page displays message from the response and keeps the Create Account form visible for correction.

Workflow: Returning user viewing account details
1) User navigates to account.html or account.html?view=profile.
2) The page reads URL parameters (view). If view is not explicitly set to create, it calls getAccountProfile.
3) If getAccountProfile returns an account object, the page sets mode=profile and populates account summary (username, email, createdAt, defaultNewsletterOptIn).
4) The Create Account form is hidden or disabled because an account already exists.
5) User can inspect account information in a read-only overview panel.

Workflow: Updating default newsletter preferences
1) From account.html?view=profile or account.html?view=preferences, user sees a preferences form that includes a control for defaultNewsletterOptIn (e.g., a checkbox or toggle).
2) The preferences form is pre-filled with the defaultNewsletterOptIn value from the current account state loaded via getAccountProfile or createAccount.
3) User changes the defaultNewsletterOptIn value and submits the preferences form.
4) The page calls updateAccountPreferences with the new defaultNewsletterOptIn value.
5) If the update is successful, the account state is updated from the response.account object and any success message is shown; the UI reflects the new opt-in status.
6) If the update fails, an error message from the response is displayed, and the form retains the last known valid value (or shows validation hints as appropriate).

Workflow: Directly opening preferences section
1) User navigates to account.html?view=preferences.
2) The page initializes state with view=preferences from the URL and calls getAccountProfile.
3) If an account exists, the page displays both the account overview and the preferences form, with focus or scroll directed to the preferences section.
4) If no account exists, the page automatically switches view to create, updates the URL to account.html?view=create, and displays the Create Account form instead, because preferences cannot be edited without an account.

Workflow: Handling URL changes while on the page
1) User is on account.html and uses in-page navigation controls (e.g., tabs or links) to switch between the Create Account view and the Profile/Preferences view.
2) The page updates the URL query parameter view to either create, profile, or preferences using history APIs without a full page reload.
3) A URL-change listener updates internal UI state so that only the appropriate component(s) are visible based on the new view value.
4) The account data already in memory from getAccountProfile or createAccount is reused; no additional calls are made unless a refresh is explicitly triggered.
interactions: User clicks the 'Sign Up' link in the site header, which navigates to account.html?view=create. The page responds by showing the Create Account form and, if necessary, confirming that no account already exists via getAccountProfile.

On page load, a data-loading interaction triggers getAccountProfile (unless the URL explicitly forces view=create and a previous check already confirmed that no account exists). This interaction governs which components (create form vs profile/preferences) are visible.

User fills out and submits the Create Account form, triggering form validation and then a call to createAccount. The success or failure of this interaction updates the central account state and may update the URL (e.g., to view=profile).
User toggles the defaultNewsletterOptIn control in the preferences form and submits it, triggering a call to updateAccountPreferences and updating the account state on success.

User navigates between logical sections (e.g., profile vs preferences) via in-page navigation controls. Clicks on these controls update the view query parameter in the URL and cause the state-router logic to show or hide relevant components.

User refreshes the browser or shares the account.html URL with specific parameters such as view=preferences. The page reads the URL parameters and reconstructs the state (which section to show) by calling getAccountProfile again and then applying the view mode from the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters to control which logical section of the Account & Preferences page is active and how initial data loads are performed. The primary query parameter is `view`, with supported values such as `create`, `profile`, and `preferences`. If `view=create`, the page prioritizes rendering the Create Account form and may still call getAccountProfile once to confirm that an account does not already exist; if an account exists, the page can redirect state to `view=profile` to avoid creating multiple accounts. If `view=profile` or `view=preferences`, the page calls getAccountProfile to load account data; if no account is returned, the state automatically switches to `view=create` and updates the URL so that the user is guided to create an account first. When no `view` parameter is specified, the page defaults to an `auto` behavior: it calls getAccountProfile and sets `view=profile` if an account exists, or `view=create` if not, and optionally updates the URL to reflect the resolved view. All subsequent navigation inside the page (e.g., switching from profile to preferences) updates the `view` parameter without reloading the page. No state is stored in localStorage; transient view state is kept in memory and can always be reconstructed from the combination of the URL parameters and a fresh call to getAccountProfile.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (primarily `view`) on initialization and on URL changes, decides whether the page is in create, profile, or preferences mode, triggers the initial getAccountProfile call when needed, and controls which child components are rendered or visible based on the resolved mode.

Encapsulates the call to getAccountProfile, retrieves the current account object, and exposes it to other components via shared state. Handles loading and error states and communicates results back to the account-state-router.

Displays input fields for username, email, and password labeled as 'Create Account'. Validates input, calls createAccount on submit, and updates the account state and URL (to a profile-oriented view) when the account is successfully created. If an account already exists, this component can be disabled or hidden based on signals from the account-state-router.

Shows read-only account information based on the loaded account object, including username, email, createdAt, and the current defaultNewsletterOptIn status. This panel is visible when the resolved view mode is profile or preferences and an account exists.

Displays and manages basic account preferences, currently focused on the defaultNewsletterOptIn setting. Initializes the checkbox or toggle from the existing account object and calls updateAccountPreferences with the updated defaultNewsletterOptIn value on submit. On success, it refreshes the bound account state from the response, ensuring the overview panel and other components stay consistent.
data_binding: NewsletterSubscription (conceptual linkage for default settings, not directly modified on this page)

components.functionality: Centralizes display of success, warning, and error messages originating from createAccount, getAccountProfile, and updateAccountPreferences. Subscribes to events from the form and loader components and shows contextual feedback (e.g., account creation success, preference save confirmation, or error details).
data_binding: OperationStatus (derived from createAccount and updateAccountPreferences responses)

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width central content area that likely maps to a 12-column grid with an approximate 8/4 split between main content and sidebar. The content column appears centered with equal light-grey gutters on each side.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the classic two-column blog layout: full-width site header at the top (unchanged from the rest of the site), then a fixed-width central content area centered on the page. Inside this central area, the left 2/3 functions as the main content column for account creation and preferences management, stacking elements from top to bottom (page title, feedback, loading state, forms). The right 1/3 is a sidebar column used for a compact account overview panel, analogous to the 'Author' section in the mockup. Invisible infrastructure components (state router, data loader) sit logically at the top of the main column, controlling what appears without adding additional visible chrome.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the classic two-column blog layout: full-width site header at the top (unchanged from the rest of the site), then a fixed-width central content area centered on the page. Inside this central area, the left 2/3 functions as the main content column for account creation and preferences management, stacking elements from top to bottom (page title, feedback, loading state, forms). The right 1/3 is a sidebar column used for a compact account overview panel, analogous to the 'Author' section in the mockup. Invisible infrastructure components (state router, data loader) sit logically at the top of the main column, controlling what appears without adding additional visible chrome.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content area spans roughly 70–75% of the viewport width, centered with light grey gutters on both sides. Within this, the main column occupies about two-thirds of the width and the sidebar one-third, mirroring the mockup’s 8/4 or 9/3 split. Main column components (feedback messages, loader, forms) each occupy nearly the full width of the main column, with internal padding around 16–24px. Vertical spacing between major blocks (feedback panel to forms, form to form, main column to sidebar alignment) is 24–32px, while internal spacing between related items within a block is 8–16px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium content density with an editorial, text-centric feel. Lines of text are kept relatively short thanks to the fixed-width main column. Forms are broken into clear groups with generous vertical breathing room between field groups and buttons. The sidebar overview panel remains visually light and compact, with concise text and comfortable line spacing, so it supports rather than competes with the main column. Overall, the layout prioritizes comfortable reading and scanning over packing in controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the site logo in the top-left of the global header, across to the main navigation, then down into the page title at the top of the main column. From there, attention drops immediately to the feedback/status band and any loading indication, then into the primary form (account creation or preferences). As users scroll down, they move through the main vertical stack of forms. The sidebar account overview, aligned to the right, provides a secondary focal point that users check periodically—similar to the 'Author' or 'Archives' areas in the mockup—without disrupting the dominant vertical reading flow of the main column. Blue or accent-colored links and primary buttons act as small anchors along this path, echoing the subtle F-pattern from the mockup.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar full-width site header, a centered content band spans about three-quarters of the viewport, framed by soft grey gutters on the left and right. This band is divided into a wider left main column and a narrower right sidebar, just like a classic blog layout. At the top of the main column, the page title 'Account & Preferences' (not a separate component) sets the context in a clean, text-first style. Immediately below, a slim feedback/status strip and any temporary loading indicator occupy the full width of the main column, separated from the title and the forms by comfortable 16–24px gaps. The primary interactive area—first the create-account form and, once created, the preferences form—sits directly beneath, each in its own block with subtle padding and plenty of space between groups of fields, mirroring the calm, editorial rhythm of a blog post rather than a dense app UI. On the right, starting near the same vertical height as the top of the forms, the account overview panel fills the sidebar width, acting like the mockup’s author panel: a compact, text-centric summary that remains visually secondary to the main column. As the user scrolls, the main column content drives the experience, while the sidebar remains a light, supportive presence. On narrower screens, the two columns collapse into a single vertical stack, preserving the top-to-bottom reading flow with the overview dropping below the forms, but retaining the same spacious, monochrome, accent-driven aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-state-router is positioned logically at the top of the main content column, immediately below the page title and above the visible forms, but it remains visually invisible in the layout. It occupies the full width of the main column in terms of flow, effectively acting as a controller for which primary block appears: when a user has no account, it routes to display the create-account-form in the main column; when an account exists, it routes to display the preferences-form instead (or in addition, depending on the product decision). The router does not introduce any extra padding or margins beyond what the visible forms already use; instead, it ensures that at any given time only the appropriate form component is rendered in the reserved space beneath the feedback/status area. On small screens, its behavior remains the same, continuing to control visibility of the forms in a single, vertical stack without altering the spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that participates in the document flow at the top of the main column without taking up its own perceivable space; it conditionally mounts or unmounts the create-account-form and preferences-form in the same main-column area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-profile-loader sits in the main column directly beneath the page title and above the feedback/status band and forms, sharing the same left alignment and width as the text content. When active, it appears as a slim inline loading row or lightweight placeholder block that spans nearly 100% of the main column width, with 16–24px vertical padding and minimal height—similar in visual weight to the date/comments line or a short text snippet beneath a blog title. It pushes the feedback/messages panel and forms downward while visible but keeps at least 16px of vertical breathing room above and below. When loading completes, the loader collapses completely, reclaiming its vertical space so that the feedback/status panel or forms sit directly beneath the title with no residual gap. On narrow screens, it simply becomes a full-width row above the forms, preserving the same vertical spacing and subtle presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the main column flow when loading, expanding to a short block with padding, then collapses to zero height when idle. It does not overlay content; instead, it gently shifts the forms downward while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The create-account-form occupies the primary content area in the left main column, positioned below any active loader and the feedback/status band, with a 24–32px vertical gap separating it from the component above. It is left-aligned and spans almost the full width of the main column (roughly two-thirds of the central content area), with 16–24px internal padding on all sides to create a clear, self-contained block—akin to a well-structured article section. Form fields are arranged in a single column, each full-width within the main column, with 8–12px vertical spacing between fields and 16–24px between logical field groups. The primary submit button sits at the bottom left of the form, aligned with the form fields, with about 16px of space above it. A secondary link or text (such as 'Already have an account?') would sit beneath or beside it, using the accent color similarly to links in the blog layout. There is a 32px margin below the form before any subsequent content (such as the preferences-form if both are shown in sequence) to preserve the spacious, editorial vertical rhythm. On smaller screens, the form stretches to nearly full screen width, but the vertical spacing and padding remain consistent so it still feels like a calm reading column rather than a compressed utility panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally within the main column. Visibility is controlled by the account-state-router so that it only appears when appropriate; otherwise, its space collapses and the preferences-form moves up into its place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-overview-panel is positioned in the right-hand sidebar column, aligned to the top so that its heading begins roughly level with the top of the main forms in the left column. It fills the full width of the sidebar (about one-third of the central content area), with 16–24px of internal padding and a subtle border or light background tint to distinguish it as a self-contained block, similar in feel to the 'Author' panel in the original mockup. Within the panel, the heading sits at the top left, followed by a short vertical stack of summary items (e.g., username, email, account status) with 4–8px spacing between lines and 16px between different sections of information. The overall height of the panel is modest, ensuring it appears lighter and shorter than the main column content so the user’s eye is naturally drawn to the larger forms on the left. There is 24–32px of vertical space beneath this panel, reserved for potential future sidebar sections, maintaining the same editorial spacing pattern. On mobile or very narrow screens, this panel drops below the main column content, remaining full-width but still visually secondary, separated from the forms by 32px of vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the sidebar that scrolls with the page; no sticky or floating behavior. On small screens, it reflows beneath the main column content as part of a single vertical stream.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferences-form shares the same main column footprint as the create-account-form—left-aligned, spanning nearly the full width of the main content column, with 16–24px of internal padding. It typically appears beneath the feedback/status band and below or instead of the create-account-form, depending on the account-state-router’s logic. If both forms are shown sequentially (e.g., account details first, then preferences), the preferences-form is placed directly under the create-account-form with a clear 32px vertical gap between them, establishing it as a distinct but related section—much like a new section of a long-form article. Fields are arranged in a single vertical column, with checkboxes and toggles (such as newsletter opt-in) aligned flush left and given 8–12px vertical spacing between each option. Group headings (e.g., 'Notifications', 'Email preferences') are treated like small subheadings with 16px of top margin, reinforcing the editorial feel. The primary save button sits at the bottom left of the form with around 16–24px of space above it. The overall visual weight is slightly lighter than the account creation form, reflecting its role as an adjustment area rather than a primary gate. On smaller screens, it expands to full width in the vertical flow but retains the same spacing, ensuring readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the main column where the account-state-router places it—either occupying the primary position when an account exists, or following the create-account-form when both are visible. It does not overlap or float; its vertical position is defined entirely by the routing state and standard document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-feedback-messages panel is a slim, horizontally-oriented status area placed in the main column directly beneath the page title (or beneath the loader if the loader is active) and above whichever form the account-state-router renders. It spans the full width of the main column, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, forming a low-height band similar in prominence to a highlighted system message or a subtle notification strip. Success, error, or informational messages appear as short lines or small paragraphs of text aligned to the left, with clear line spacing to keep them easy to read. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between this panel and the form below, so the feedback is visually connected to the subsequent action but still clearly separated. When there are no messages to show, this component collapses entirely, allowing the forms to move up so that there is no empty band left behind. On narrow screens, it remains a full-width strip above the forms, preserving its function as an immediate contextual cue before users interact with the inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands and collapses dynamically within the main column flow based on whether there are messages to display; it does not overlay content or stick to the viewport, instead pushing the forms below it downward when visible.

name: About
title: About
description: About page that loads and displays dynamic information about the blog and its creator using getAboutPageContent, and links to the contact page.

core_features: Fetch About page content (title, main body, and sections) from the getAboutPageContent interface on page load.
Render the page title and markdown-based body content returned by getAboutPageContent.
Render each section from getAboutPageContent.sections as distinct, linkable subsections.
Provide in-page navigation to jump to specific sections using URL parameters to control which section is focused.
Detect and handle clicks on internal links within the markdown content, including a 'Contact me' link that navigates to contact.html.
user_workflows:
View About information:
1. User navigates to about.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2. The page controller calls getAboutPageContent.
3. When the response arrives, the page title is updated from the returned title.
4. The main bodyMarkdown content is parsed and rendered.
5. Each item in sections is rendered as a separate section with its heading and contentMarkdown.

Deep-link to a specific section:
1. User opens about.html with an optional URL parameter, e.g., about.html?section={encoded-heading}.
2. The page controller calls getAboutPageContent.
3. After content is rendered, the controller looks for the first section whose heading matches the decoded section parameter.
4. If found, the page scrolls to that section and can mark it as focused (e.g., via a state flag used for styling).
5. If no matching section is found, the page remains at the top without error.

Navigate between sections from within the page:
1. User views the list of section links generated from getAboutPageContent.sections.
2. User clicks a section link.
3. The click handler updates the browser URL to about.html?section={encoded-heading-of-target} without reloading the entire page (if routing allows) or via a full reload.
4. The controller re-runs the section-focus logic so the clicked section becomes the focused section.

Go to Contact page from the About content:
1. User reads the rendered markdown body or sections.
2. User clicks a link that targets contact.html (such as a 'Contact me' link contained in the markdown).
3. The click handler allows navigation to contact.html (optionally appending a URL parameter like ?source=about if desired by the overall site routing).
4. The browser loads contact.html according to the site’s navigation rules.
interactions: On page load: trigger a call to getAboutPageContent, then render title, bodyMarkdown, and sections.
On URL parameter change (section): re-run logic to identify and focus the corresponding section based on the returned headings.
On click of a section navigation link generated from section headings: update the URL section parameter and focus the corresponding section.

On click of an internal link within the rendered markdown content:
- If the href points to contact.html (optionally with query parameters), navigate to that URL.
- If the href points to another internal location on about.html (e.g., another section), update the URL parameters and scroll accordingly.
- If the href is external, open it following the site’s global link behavior.
On markdown render errors (e.g., invalid markdown): fail gracefully by displaying the raw text or an error-safe fallback without blocking the rest of the page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page uses URL parameters to control which section is focused when rendering dynamic content. The base URL is about.html with an optional query parameter 'section'. When about.html is loaded or when the 'section' parameter changes, the page:
1. Calls getAboutPageContent (which has no input parameters) to retrieve the current About content.
2. Renders the page title using the returned 'title' field.
3. Renders the main body using 'bodyMarkdown'.
4. Renders each section from 'sections' in order.
5. If 'section' is present in the query string, the page decodes its value and compares it to each section.heading value. The first section where heading exactly matches the decoded parameter is considered the 'focused section'.
6. The focused section is scrolled into view and may be marked with an internal focus flag used by components for functional styling or ARIA attributes.
7. If there is no 'section' parameter or no matching heading, no section is marked focused and the page simply shows all sections from the top.
No content or settings are stored in localStorage; all state is derived from the getAboutPageContent response and the current URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching from getAboutPageContent, manages URL parameter-based state (such as which section is focused), and passes the resulting data and state down to child components.
Invokes getAboutPageContent once the page controller initializes, handles loading and error states, and exposes the returned data to other components.
Displays the About page title using the 'title' field returned by getAboutPageContent, and can also be used for document.title synchronization.
Renders the main descriptive content of the About page from the 'bodyMarkdown' field returned by getAboutPageContent, including inline links, lists, and paragraphs.
Generates a list of navigational links to each section based on section headings from getAboutPageContent.sections, and updates the 'section' URL parameter and focused section when a link is clicked.
Iterates over getAboutPageContent.sections and renders each section's heading and markdown content. Integrates with the page's focused-section logic to scroll or highlight the active section.
Renders the contentMarkdown for each individual section, including handling internal links such as the 'Contact me' link by delegating navigation to the router or browser.

Intercepts link clicks within bodyMarkdown and section content. If a link’s target is contact.html, it initiates navigation to contact.html (optionally with query parameters such as source=about). For other links, it defers to default navigation behavior.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width central content area that likely maps to a 12-column grid with an approximate 8/4 split between main content and sidebar. The content column appears centered with equal light-grey gutters on each side.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page uses the same classic two-column blog layout as the mockup. Below the global full-width site header (logo + nav, outside this spec), the page content sits inside a fixed-width central container. Inside this container, the left 2/3 is the main About content column and the right 1/3 is a lighter sidebar. The main column flows top-to-bottom: page title at the top, then a slim internal navigation row (mirroring the date/comments row from the mockup), followed by the main About markdown content and any section-specific markdown content. The sidebar aligns to the right and stacks its elements vertically, starting with a sections list (functionally similar to the Archives list) and ending with the Contact link area.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page uses the same classic two-column blog layout as the mockup. Below the global full-width site header (logo + nav, outside this spec), the page content sits inside a fixed-width central container. Inside this container, the left 2/3 is the main About content column and the right 1/3 is a lighter sidebar. The main column flows top-to-bottom: page title at the top, then a slim internal navigation row (mirroring the date/comments row from the mockup), followed by the main About markdown content and any section-specific markdown content. The sidebar aligns to the right and stacks its elements vertically, starting with a sections list (functionally similar to the Archives list) and ending with the Contact link area.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content container spans roughly 70–75% of the viewport width and is centered, leaving soft light-grey gutters on both sides. Within this container, the main About column takes about 65–70% of the container width, with the sidebar occupying the remaining 30–35%. A consistent gutter of approximately 32px separates the main column from the sidebar. Vertical spacing follows the 8px-based scale: 48–64px from the global header to the page title, 8–16px between title and the internal navigation row, 16–24px between that row and the main body markdown, and 24–32px between major content blocks and section areas.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density remains comfortably low to medium, similar to an editorial blog article. The main column emphasizes readability: relatively short line lengths, generous line height, and clear paragraph breaks. Sidebar content is moderately dense but not cramped: section links are stacked with 4–8px vertical spacing, and the entire sidebar has internal padding of 16–24px to keep it airy. Headings and section separations in both columns use 16–24px vertical gaps to maintain the relaxed, text-centric feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s vertical reading pattern with a slight F-pattern bias. The eye comes from the global header into the About page title at the top-left of the content container, then moves horizontally across the slim internal navigation row just beneath it, similar to scanning a date/comments line. From there, attention drops down through the main About markdown content. Periodically, the reader’s gaze is drawn to the right sidebar where the sections list and contact link provide secondary navigation anchors, before returning to the main reading column. Standard blue links or a single accent color punctuate both columns as small visual anchors without overpowering the text.

layout.overall_description: The About page appears as a calm, editorial-style layout centered on the screen. Below the site’s full-width header, a fixed-width content panel stretches across roughly three-quarters of the viewport, framed by soft, neutral side gutters. Within this panel, the main About content forms a wide left column, clearly dominant and built for reading, while a slimmer sidebar sits to the right as a secondary column. At the top of the main column, the About page title establishes hierarchy. Immediately beneath it, a single subtle row of internal navigation links echoes the look and position of a blog post’s date and comments line from the mockup. Below, the primary About text unfolds, rendered in clean, readable typography with comfortable line spacing and generous margins. Further down in the same column, section-specific content appears, allowing deeper dives into different aspects of the blog and its creator without breaking the vertical reading rhythm. In the right sidebar, a list of About sections, styled like an archive list, offers quick access points and a light structural overview. At the bottom of this sidebar, a simple Contact call-to-action sits with extra breathing room, linking into the site’s contact page. Everything scrolls together in a straightforward, linear fashion, maintaining the understated, monochrome-first aesthetic with a single accent color reserved for links and key interactive elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as an invisible structural wrapper for the About page, positioned directly below the global site header. It defines a central content container that is horizontally centered on the viewport and spans approximately 70–75% of the viewport width, leaving equal light-grey gutters on the left and right. Vertically, there is a 48–64px gap from the bottom edge of the global header to the top of the About page title area inside this controller. Within this container, the controller establishes a two-column layout: a main content column on the left that occupies roughly 65–70% of the container’s width and a sidebar on the right that occupies the remaining 30–35%, separated by a fixed horizontal gap of about 32px. All child components on this page (title header, markdown content, internal navigation, section list, section markdown, and contact link area) are positioned within this controller, following a linear top-to-bottom flow in each column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the fixed-width, centered layout and two-column grid; it scrolls with the page and does not have any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader occupies the main visual area of the About page whenever data is being fetched. It is anchored within the about-page-controller’s central container and is horizontally centered within the main content column. When active, it sits in the space where the About title, body markdown, and section markdown would normally appear, starting roughly 48–64px below the global header. It extends across the full width of the main content column (about 65–70% of the central container width), with 16–24px internal padding on all sides to maintain the airy editorial feel. If a loading skeleton or spinner is used, it should be vertically centered within the initial viewport height of the main column, leaving the sidebar either empty or displaying minimal placeholder content to keep the main column visually dominant. Once data is loaded, this loader collapses entirely and relinquishes its space to the actual About content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the main content column area during data fetching, then disappears from the layout; it scrolls normally if the page height exceeds the viewport while loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The About title header is positioned at the top-left of the central content container, inside the main content column defined by the page controller. It begins about 48–64px below the global header, creating a clear separation between site-level navigation and page-level content. The title spans the full width of the main column (roughly 65–70% of the central container), left-aligned, and is the first prominent text users see. There is no sidebar content to its left or above; the sidebar begins aligned roughly with or slightly below the baseline of the line immediately beneath this title. Below the title, there is a vertical gap of 8–16px before the internal section navigation line, mirroring the spacing between a post title and a date/comments line in the mockup. Additional vertical space of 16–24px separates the title area from the main body markdown that follows, maintaining the spacious editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main column; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary About body markdown renderer sits directly beneath the title header and the slim internal navigation row in the main content column. It starts approximately 16–24px below the bottom edge of the internal navigation line, spanning the full width of the main column (about 65–70% of the central container). The text lines are constrained to this column, yielding relatively short, readable line lengths consistent with the mockup’s editorial style. Paragraphs are separated by 8–16px vertical spacing, with headings and key subsections spaced 16–24px apart. Standard link styling uses the site’s single accent color (e.g., blue) to create subtle visual anchors. The right sidebar begins to the immediate right of this markdown area, separated by a ~32px gutter, so the reader’s main vertical scanning path stays within this markdown block, occasionally glancing right to the sidebar. A vertical margin of 24–32px at the bottom of this block separates it from any section-specific markdown content that follows in the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in normal document order within the main column and expands vertically based on content length; it scrolls as the primary reading area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The internal section navigation is a slim horizontal row positioned in the main content column directly beneath the About title header, emulating the position and scale of the date-and-comments line in the mockup. It is left-aligned within the main column, occupying its full width but with its individual links clustered toward the left side, leaving open space to the right for a clean look. This navigation row starts about 8–16px below the title and uses smaller, more subdued typography than the title, with links styled in the accent color and separated by 16–24px of horizontal spacing. The row’s height is modest, preserving the text-centric feel and not competing visually with the title. A 16–24px gap separates the bottom of this row from the start of the about-body-markdown content. The sidebar column to the right begins vertically around the same height or slightly lower than this navigation row, reinforcing the page’s slight F-pattern: title, nav row, then a scan across to the sidebar’s top edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky navigation row that scrolls away with the rest of the main content; it does not remain fixed as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section list occupies the upper part of the right-hand sidebar column and aligns vertically so that its heading (if present) begins roughly in line with, or slightly below, the internal navigation row under the About title. The sidebar column itself sits to the immediate right of the main column, separated by a consistent gutter of about 32px, and occupies approximately 30–35% of the central container width. The section list block has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, creating a light, boxed-in feel similar to the Author/Archives sections in the mockup. Within this block, the list heading is at the top, followed by a vertical stack of section links (e.g., “About the blog,” “About the reviewer,” “Philosophy,” etc.). Each list item is left-aligned and uses regular body text size, with 4–8px vertical spacing between items to achieve a moderately dense but not cramped appearance. The block’s bottom edge is separated by 24px or more from any subsequent sidebar content, such as the contact link area, preserving clear section boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked content within the sidebar; it scrolls together with the main content and does not pin or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-specific markdown renderer sits in the main content column below the primary About body markdown, functioning as an area for detailed content about whichever section the user has navigated to (via the internal nav row or sidebar section list). It begins approximately 24–32px beneath the end of the about-body-markdown block, preserving a clear visual separation that feels like moving from an introduction into deeper sections. This renderer spans the full width of the main column (65–70% of the central container) and follows the same typographic rules: readable line length, 8–16px spacing between paragraphs, and 16–24px between major headings or subsections. If multiple subsections are rendered, they are stacked vertically with consistent spacing, mirroring how subsequent paragraphs would follow the main article content in a blog post. The right sidebar remains visible alongside this renderer, so as the user scrolls through section content, the section list and contact link in the sidebar continue to provide contextual navigation and calls to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically as needed within the main column; appears after the introductory markdown and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact link handler is associated with a visually modest but clearly identifiable Contact link, which is placed in the lower portion of the sidebar column to create a distinct, light call-to-action zone. Visually, the contact link appears as a standard text link or small text button block positioned beneath the section list block, separated from it by a generous 24–32px vertical gap to avoid visual clutter. The contact link is left-aligned within the sidebar and respects the sidebar’s internal padding of 16–24px. It does not attempt to dominate the sidebar; instead, it acts as a clean, single-line anchor in the accent color that stands out against the mostly monochrome text. The handler captures interactions with this link (and optionally any inline ‘Contact’ mentions within the main or section markdown content) but does not introduce additional visible chrome beyond the link itself. As the user scrolls past the main content, this contact link scrolls naturally along with the sidebar content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Attaches to inline or small-block contact links, primarily in the sidebar’s lower area; it scrolls with the sidebar and does not have fixed or sticky behavior.
